## Story 00504
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @CorridorCrew
- **Source:** yt_transcript_short_film
- **Video ID:** 

Imagine you're a VFX artist and your boss gives you an impossible green screen shot. You need to remove the background perfectly or you're fired. What do you do? You may think you could just color pick the background and tell the computer to turn any green pixels transparent, but then you'd get rid of parts of the image that you don't want to remove and you'd be fired. Maybe you could use an AI roto tool like magic mask or roto brush, but then any translucent materials like veils or this glass would be fully solid instead, which would also get you fired. Until now, the only other ways you could accomplish this task is by masking out every individual piece of the shot frame by frame by hand. This will take weeks. That's too long for your boss and you'd be fired once more. Fortunately, there is hope for you. I just spent the last 8 weeks programming my own solution to this problem. This is Corridor Key, a free open- source program that takes any green screen shot you give it and it perfectly separates the background from the foreground while also removing any green screen from translucent objects. [music] It works by showing a custommade neural network an image. Then the network guesses what that image would look like without a background. However, in order for those guesses to be accurate, the computer needs to be taught what the right answer is. [music] To do that, I created thousands of fake green screen shots using CGI. And this allowed me to render out four different versions of each shot. I show the original image to the neural network and it makes its cutout guess. Then I show it the correct answer that I rendered out. The computer takes note of the differences and little by little it stops making the same mistakes. If you do that thousands of times, eventually the computer will get so good at its predictions that a shot that was once impossible becomes possible. And you no longer need to worry about getting fired by your weirdly hyper strict boss that hates green screens.

andor out of all of the Star Wars properties is easily the coolest and I really like their philosophy behind doing their visual effects. They really wanted to ground their effects in reality and to do that they basically shot on location for a lot of their sequences and they would end up replacing a lot of the stuff in the shot. It's what I keep calling informed intent. It's like everyone's on the same page about the composition of the shot, the way the shot should feel. Even if it's all getting replaced, it's still contributing to what they're trying to do. Uh, so basically these are just some befores and afters. Uh, and oh wow, this is a really interesting one. They shot on some sort of like industrial yard here, but then they added a bunch of digital machines matching the sort of aesthetic and lighting of the real ones there. But it just makes so much sense cuz like comping a real thing into like a full 3D shot is one of the hardest things. And that's why there's like so many janky shots like in Marvel where you can just tell like, oh, this is the green screen the movie. Essentially, this comes back to the philosophy of trying to ground everything. It doesn't necessarily mean doing everything practically. That's a misunderstanding. There's something to your movie like actually having happened. You filmed it. It existed in a certain moment in time. Knowing that these things exist in the real world is kind of it's just kind of cool. Exciting.

So, a racetrack UFO is another way of describing a satellite when it's catching the sun from behind the horizon at that exact angle where it glints and then shines a light to a viewer as they're standing on the Earth. A lot of them oriented facing the Earth and as it's coming over the Earth, that little flat satellite will perfectly capture the sun and glint it off that satellite and right into your eye, which leads the satellite going dark, very bright, and then going dark again. If you actually spin a disc and you have points on the edge of that disc, the points will appear to get closer together at the sides of that disc when you have perspective coming into play. But in this case, a spinning disc, all the lights stay evenly spaced apart as they come in and out of the light. So it's not a spinning disc. It's actually satellites moving in a line. And here's proof. Mick West has built this tool called SITRE. And you can see the satellites in a live flight pattern recreating the moment that you've seen for all these racetrack UFO sightings. ## Story 00525
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @CorridorCrew
- **Source:** yt_transcript_short_film
- **Video ID:** 

Development for Lord of the Rings started in 1997. VFX hadn't taken over quite yet. So, in order to make a bunch of 5 foot something actors appear 3-foot something, Peter Jackson tried everything. Little people as scale doubles, big people as scale doubles, averagesized people dressed as big people as scale doubles. They had one of the earliest face replacements, one of the earliest digles, duplicate sets, chroma key compositing with a motion controlled camera and an object handoff between subjects, good old-fashioned dummies, and of course, forced perspective. Of all the techniques used in these movies, this one's my favorite. [clears throat] Here's how it works. So, Jordan and I are [music] both 10 feet away from the camera. So, for him to be half the size of me, he's got to go twice as far. This formula works at any scale ratio. And believe it or not, any lens. This is dwarf. This is leprechaun. And this [music] is hobbit. Now, we need to eliminate the depth cues. Those are the subtle hints that tell your brain that Jordan isn't small. He's just far away. Depth cue number one is the height of our feet relative to each other. You see, our feet give away the ground plane, which gives away the depth. So, we just need to bring in a secret weapon, [music] an elevated platform. The next depth cue we got to tackle is depth of field. I'm all out of focus. >> So, I'm going to stop down the aperture to expand the depth of field. And now, I have to compensate for the loss of light. [music] >> Now, I can't just look back at Jordan because then that gives away that he's behind me. So, I need to look right here, and Jordan's going to look there. Is this a prom picture? But so long as we maintain these ey lines, it should look like we're making [music] eye contact. At this point, we've removed all the cues that break the illusion. So now, let's add some cues that sell the illusion. Your brain instinctively uses familiar objects for scale reference. So, we can hack that instinct with a scale prop. >> Another cue that hacks your depth perception is interaction. Now, if I'm very careful, I can pan and tilt the camera without breaking the illusion. And that's because my camera is rotating around the nodal [music] point. That's the spot inside the lens where the light converges. So, the camera's rotating, but it's not really moving. And the same is true for all the examples we've looked at so far. [music] Every single one of them is nodally locked. And the reason for that is a depth cue we haven't talked about yet. A giveaway so powerful it instantly breaks any perspective illusion. Parallax. As your vantage point moves through space, close objects appear to move faster than far objects. That is why when doing force perspective, you must never ever move the camera through space. Then one day, an underdog studio buys a pitch from an underdog filmmaker from an underdog island nation to adapt the most important underdog story of all time. This crew of underdogs looked at a hundred years of nodally locked cameras and they didn't see a limitation. They saw a challenge. If they could just find a way to move the camera through space, they'd have a force perspective shot unlike [music] anything anyone had ever seen.

This crazy movie came out 20 years ago and this was considered unfilmable. Sin City is a series of graphic novels written by Frank Miller. It's a black and white series, very heavy with the silhouettes and dripping in style. How do you make a movie out of that? At the time when this was made, it was one of the first movies shot entirely on a digital camera and mostly on a green screen background. With color movies, their color is what helps separate them from the background. But when you lose that color, he needed to edgelight all of the actors to get their silhouettes to stand out when on a black background. So, as an example, in the comic, this guy is covered in bandages. And because of the very unique style, he's completely black as a silhouette, but the bandages stand out as white. How do you achieve something like that for real? Oh, you would need some sort of reflective. Reflective tape. Is it reflective or is it fluorescent? Fluorescent tape. Basically, they got this fluorescent tape cuz they needed those bandages to show up in the shadows by having like a little bit of a black light on it. They glow. It's glowing just enough for them to take a mat out of it. So, they cut out the green screen and now they have the mat of the actor here. And now they'll do an additional mat where they're basically color keying out the red of the tape and they're able to isolate that stuff, add that as a separate pass to make it much more punchy. This is an example of a studio green screen set being used with intent.

[Music] CG smoke bomb right in front of camera. This is daring. Why does he look so CG? I think it's because there's a tremendous amount of compositing going on because of all the CG smoke. One of the biggest challenges with doing all the smoke like this is having the people composited at the correct depth within a smoke shot. When you render out smoke, it's a 2D element that's either in front of or behind your actors. And if you want to have them be sort of faded out, then you have to render out what's called a Zpass or do some sort of depth mapping on your scene so you can actually composite it correctly. You're actually rendering out far more than you're actually seeing when it comes to the smoke. You're having to render out all of the slices within it. Notice how every now and then there's a little person that like walks across the screen and like conveniently crosses both the bottom and the top of frame, left to right. If you want to make a great stopmotion animation, I've got some pro tips for you. Step one is to grab some of your Lego bricks, specifically some of these base plates. You can use these to animate your characters on, but you don't want these sliding around. So, my first pro tip is to lock this down. I'm using a little bit of sticky tack on each of these corners. And now, my base plates are not going anywhere. Same thing goes for your camera. I recommend using a phone mount to lock it down as well. You don't want your camera to move. Oh, but you see that wobble? I don't want that either. So, pro tip, use the timer function on the camera to actually give the phone a few seconds to settle before it takes a picture. No more wobble. But why does my picture look all dark and muddy? That's because we need some light. And you can use any light, including like a simple lamp you've got lying around. But if you want to get extra fancy, consider putting your light behind your set. This is what's called an edge light, and makes your stuff look extra cinematic. So, this is what my scene looks like without the lights. And this is what it looks like with the lights. Notice how it looks so much better. Now, if I only have three words of advice for any filmmaking, let alone stop motion, it is add more light. And now I'm ready to animate. I move my figure. I take a photo. I move my figure a little bit more. I take another photo. And if I ever need any help, I turn on onion skinning. This is a great feature. Check this out. This shows me the live view overlaid on the previous picture taken so I know exactly how far to move my character before I take the picture. But what if I want my character to not move? What if he's done with his motion and he needs to pause? Well, I can just take another identical photo, or I can add a pause anywhere I want by pressing the duplicate frame button. The more duplicates I add, the more he's going to pause. My final pro tip is just to be patient. Stopotion takes a while, so relax, settle in, and have fun. And that is probably the most important thing we learned from our friends over at Ardman. They're the creators of Wallace Grommet. They are legends. And if you love Ardman and Lego, then I've got some great news. Ardman just made a short film using Lego bricks, but they need your help to finish it. Just download the Lego Play app and join the LEGO Insiders Club to share your stopmotion creativity. But how do you get started?

Oh yeah, there's the mask. Pop pop pop. >> Yeah. >> Yeah. You see those stars disappearing? >> 2001 of Space Odyssey. Stanley Kubri filmed this whole movie to make it seem like it was a first generation film pass. >> What does that mean? >> Is like whatever light hits the film for the first time, that's what you get at the end. But if you're doing visual effects, doing hold out mats, and you're filming stuff through that to expose another piece of film, the quality degrades every time you do that pass. It's like doing a photocopy of a photocopy of a photocopy. A colorist on YouTube was able to get the HDR version of this movie that has way more dynamic range in it. And he essentially just boosted the exposure on all the shots from the movie so that we can now actually see the original matte lines. >> Oh, cool. >> Yeah. Here we go. >> Oh yeah, there's the mask. Pop pop pop. >> Yeah. You see those stars disappearing? But the main thing they did differently here is that they would take the original 65 mm print and create three different masters that are black and white film strips. So they would take that shot and they would project that through a red filter. So only the red light is exposing that black and white film and then the blue light and then the green light. And when you recombine those you get a color image. But the reason why they did that is because black and white film has much better grain, much smaller grain crystals. That was how they maintained that first generation quality look. >> That's crazy. I had never heard that before.

## Story 00639
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @DaFluffyPotato
- **Source:** yt_transcript_pixel_story
- **Video ID:** 

I made this pixel planet renderer with just shaders and no input geometry 2D shaders mostly just give you the pixel coordinates so we can Mark out the planet by checking the distance to the center of the screen then we convert the pixel coordinates to the 3D location on the sphere using the distance formula again to find the Z component next we look up the coordinate in 3D noise to get the height of the surface which we can map to a color palette to generate new terrain with water beaches and land if we do this again for a planet with a larger radius and map everything above with a noise threshold to White and keep the background for everything below we get clouds since the noise map coordinates are 3D we can perform matrix multiplication with a transformation matrix containing a rotational transform to rotate the noise lookup coordinates and create the appearance of the planet rotating we can also offset the cloud lookup by the time to make the cloud shift finally we can take the normal of the sphere and use the Fong formula to get the light level with a moving light source so we can look up the light level in this palette and dither texture for fancy lighting swapping palettes and constants gives us this random Planet generator

## Story 00640
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @DaFluffyPotato
- **Source:** yt_transcript_pixel_story
- **Video ID:** 

I've been working on an online multiplayer VR shooter written in Python for a while now. Python and VR aren't really known for mixing, which is why my game, Gunlaw VR, dominates the search results for Python VR. As a result, I've largely had to blaze me on trail when it comes to software architecture. And to be clear, with exception to shaders and build tools, every bit of Gunsaw's code is written entirely in standard Python. I figured it'd be helpful to some people to break it down. Open XR is the standard cross-platform API for interacting with nearly every modern VR device. Pyen XR is a module that provides what we call bindings which implement Python functions that call open XR functions under the hood. This gives the game fairly direct access to input like the position of your eyes or your hands and the buttons being pressed. Rendering to the headset is a bit more complicated. I primarily use modern GL for rendering which is an object-oriented interface for the graphics library OpenGL which makes use of system resources like the GPU to render stuff. It starts with the eye positions I get from Open XR, which I use to place cameras in my implementation of a 3D game engine to render a scene in Modern GL using a lot of 3D math running on PIGLM, a math library. The resulting scenes for the eyes are OpenGL resources representing flat images the eyes will see. In order to get these images into the headset, I have to do a little bit of code glue in Pyonggl, which are the more raw and less pretty bindings for OpenGL to pass what are called frame buffers to Open XR and eventually to the VR headset so that the players can see the scene. Most VR games also have a window on your PC's desktop so others can watch what you're doing or you can screen record what's happening in the game. To do this, I had to do a bit of code glue with Py OpenGL to pass the scene from the right eye to PIGLFW, which are the bindings for GLFW, which gives me an interface to an actual application window in Windows, so I can show the game on the desktop. If you notice, I've also got Pygame over here. It's actually not connected to any low-level resources. In this use case, I purely use it to render UI elements like the watch face, bomb screen, sites, menus, etc. The rendered textures are converted in software to use the data format for OpenGL textures without using any of PI games features for low-level graphics interfaces. Those textures are used during the main world rendering I do with modern GL. Over on the far left, I've listed PI wavefront which is the module I use to parse OBG 3D module data. All the way on the other side, we can see PI open AL, the Python bindings for OpenAL, basically the audio equivalent of OpenGL, which standardizes the interface to machine audio interfaces. Finally, since GunsVR is an online multiplayer game, there's a large net code component. I've written my own framework, also purely in Python, called Shopnet. So, the game client net code and server instance can be written in Python. Shnet uses the built-in Python sockets library to interact with network interfaces and send data over the internet. I've pinned the old spaghetti open source version of this game in the comments if you want to look into making your own VR games with Python.

svgs are cool but even cooler when they look like they're being drawn in real time step one create an svg path that is styled with a stroke and then export it to your html then add an id to the path you want to animate step two add a dash to the stroke using the dash array property but make the dash the same length as the path itself you can get the exact length of the path by opening up the svg in the browser then grab the path with the id and call get total length step 3 make the dash invisible by moving it or offsetting it by the same length step four create a keyframes animation that animates the dash offset back to zero assign it to the path and make sure the fill mode is forwards so that it ends with the fully drawn svg and that is how a professional web developer signs their name

interviewer what is an abstract syntax tree you when we write code we want to use text that is as concise as possible but things like compilers want a lot more information to fully understand your code so first we perform lexical analysis to tokenize all the different parts of the code like variables operators functions and so on it's like finding all the nouns and verbs in a sentence we then take that list of tokens and parse it into a tree that represents the structure of the code this abstract syntax tree can then be safely used by compilers to generate a different type of code like turn typescript code into javascript or c plus plus into machine code

I always thought computers were better at math and people until I tried to add 0.1 to 0.2 and got an answer of 0.34 what the hell JavaScript is just a toy language so I figured it was a bug and tried the same thing in Python but it also gave me the same wrong answer then I discovered that these languages aren't actually broken they just do floating Point arithmetic computers have a limited amount of memory and need to make a trade-off between range and Precision in JavaScript all numbers must be stored within 64 bits that means we can have integer is accurate up to 15 digits and a Max of 17 numbers after the decimal point it's called a floating point because there are no fixed number of digits before or after the decimal point allowing it to represent a wide range of numbers both big and small the problem is that computers use a base 2 system binary while humans use a base 10 system that leads to rounding errors when all the memory has been used up and that's why your computer sucks at math

jpeg PNG webp GIF and SVG what's the difference between these image formats let's simplify the equation by separating the raster formats from the vector formats a raster or bitmap is like a grid of pixels best suited for complex pictures like the ones you take on your camera but they require lots of memory at high resolution and lose their Clarity when scaled up JPEG and PNG are the ogs from the 90s jpeg is smaller because it doesn't support transparency and uses lossy compression while PNG has transparency with lossless compression GIF not GIF is similar but can also combine frames to make animations webp is a new format from Google that has the best of all worlds with smaller transparent animatable images on the other side we have vector image formats that are good for flat Graphics like logos and icons because they use geometry and code to represent the image which means it can be scaled to any size without losing resolution hence the name scalable vector graphics

## Story 00688
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @Fireship
- **Source:** yt_transcript_ai_creator
- **Video ID:** 

if you want to get a job as a programmer you need to know Big O imagine you have a shuffled deck of cards if you want to pop one card off the stack that's extremely fast or o of one constant time but if you then want to count the cards you'll need to Loop over the entire deck resulting in O of n or linear time but now let's imagine we need to sort the deck in perfect order that's going to require a strategy or algorithm like bubble sort where we Loop over the deck repeatedly and swap out adjacent cards until the entire thing is perfectly sorted worst case we Loop over it 52 times giving us o of n^ s or quadratic time alternatively if you're stupid you could throw the cards up in the air and hope that they land perfectly sorted on the ground that's called BOGO sort and gives you o of n factorial the worst possible time complexity but now we want to find the eight of hearts in the sorted deck instead of looping through every card you could do a binary search by splitting the deck in the middle if the shown card is greater than nine go left otherwise go right continue that process to cut your work in half after every step thus giving you o of log n or logarithmic time

Have you ever looked at Earth and you were like, "This is ghetto as hell. I'm out." Well, that's exactly what these people did when they built structures on elevated surfaces so that nobody could touch them. If you go to the central Mandandalay regions of Myanmar, you can find Tang Kalat, a Buddhist monastery that is built on top of a volcanic plug. It sits about 737 m above the town below and it has 777 steps to get up. Maybe you're more into the Mediterranean atmosphere. Go to Meteora, Greece. This is basically the Greek Orthodox hermit dwelling capital of Greece. It's not just one. It's like multiple pillars where there are just isolated monasteries. Maybe you want more of a community in a more historical region. Well, then look no further than the northern Kurdistan regions of Iraq because there you can find on an isolated plateau the town of Amadia. This isolated town dates back to like 3000 BC. That's like the Bronze Age. And it's it used to be a citadel fortress and now it's just a town. So, if you still want to have like a social life but be isolated, this is probably your best bet. Or maybe you're all about that island life. Go to Sri Lanka and you will find Sigirya or the Lion's Rock. It was originally built as a palace for King Kashyapa I in the fifth century and then it became a Buddhist monastery and now it's an iconic ruin site that you got to take a bunch of stairs to get up to. Maybe you're looking for something a little bit more tranquil and inland. Well, if you go to the Guo province of China, oh, well, that sounds like you're going to need to visit the Fun Jing Shan Temple. This is a pilgrimage site for many Chinese Buddhists, and it was built in the Ming Dynasty. Two temples on twin pillars. Maybe you don't want anything. and you just want to be by yourself, go to the country of Georgia because you will find the famous Katsky pillar built in the 9th century. On top of the 40 m high pillar is a single church in a living unit. All of it is only accessible by a ladder. Now, let's say you want to live on a pillar, but you don't want to go on the top. You want to have a secret entrance on the side. Go to the northern highlands region of Ethiopia. Find the narrow pathway with no railing so you could fall and die at any moment. and you will find the famous Abuna Yamata Church, Ethiopia's most difficult to access church, which was carved and huned from the entire rock side. Or let's just say all of this is too much for you and you don't want anybody to access you, then look no further than the drangar lighthouse just off the coast of that what's it like glacier, whatever it's called. This is quite possibly the most inaccessible lighthouse on Earth. You can't even sail at the bottom and climb up because the waters are too choppy. Your only option is to take a helicopter. The lighthouse is only like 4 m tall and they have like a landing pad. Nobody actually lives there, but do they do have maintenance people that come in once in a while? But yeah, this is probably like your best bet for not dealing with anybody. I think the band Calo did a music VIDEO HERE. AND WAY down we go. Oh. Oh. Anyway, so there you go. Isolated Pillar Places. Which one's your favorite? Which ones did I miss? Let me know.

## Story 00699
- **Weight:** 3

- **Video ID:** 

Imagine you have a neighbor. You go up to them and you're like, "Why hello neighbor. HOW ARE YOU?" THE Whoa there, Johnny. Looks like you two need a lingua frana, aka a language that is used on a national scale to connect people groups that otherwise would not understand each other. Around the world, there are many lingua frankas. Usually, they pertain to whatever dominant language there is in the area or a former colonial language, you know, like English, Russian, even Arabic is considered colonial. But did you know that across the world there are fusion lingua frana that are sort of reconstructed standardized newish languages that are used for people to intercommunicate. For example, Swahili or Kiswuahili originally based off of the Zanzibbar dialect of Kyunguja. Later on, it adopted a ton of loan words about 40% from Arabic. In fact, the word Swahili even comes from Arabic Swahil coast. It even took from others like Persian, Hindustani, English, Portuguese, and then it standardized itself and spread across all of East Africa. Today it's official in Tanzania, Kenya and Uganda and recognized regionally in all the other countries around it. Whereas in Indonesia they have Bahasa Indonesia which is basically a standardized version of the real/JOR Malay dialect. It also has lexical expansion words that include Dutch and Arabic and Sanskrit. In the Central African Republic and DRC they have Sango and Lingala. These are the only native Creole/Pigeon languages that are used as lingua frankas and official languages in the countries. Sango is a creole that's based off of the Inbandi languages that are found off of the Ubangi River. And Lingala was a pigeon based off of the Bangi language that was used for all these areas on the rivers for trade, mostly on the Congo River. It incorporated lots of words from other Bontu languages. And over time, more people grew up speaking it natively, and now it is considered a native language of the Congo, not a pigeon. In various parts of Melanesia, they have their own pigeon languages. For example, in Papanukini, they have to piss, which is a creole that mixes Papuan languages with English. Whereas in Vanuatu, they have Bislama, which is a French-based creole. Both of them are official languages in their respective countries, whereas Solomon Islands has Solomon Islands pigeon, but it's not official. Only English is official. Then we get to Surinam or Surinama, the only Dutch-speaking country in all of South America. I got to be careful with the map that I use because these are both disputed areas. Surinam is one of the most linguistically unique places on Earth because unlike many other places in the Americas, a lot of the people here in diaspora maintains the mother tongue of the places that their ancestors came from. but like with a Caribbean dialect. So they have like Japanese people that speak like Caribbean Japanese and when they go to Indonesia they like have trouble understanding but it's still Japanese. You know what I mean? Now Dutch is the official language. However, almost everybody here learns how to speak surongo. Surinono is actually an English-based creole that mixes West African words. A native English speaker might be able to listen really hard and understand like maybe half of it. Like Miaaka is I am walking. IT'S HOW THEY TALK HERE. THERE'S A LOT OF other places I could have mentioned but those are just a few. What are some other ones that you KNOW OF? TELL ME.

## Story 00701
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @GeographyNow
- **Source:** yt_transcript_worldbuilding
- **Video ID:** 

Let's talk about insular East Africa. First up, it's made of four countries: Mauritius, Seychelles, Comoros, and Madagascar. You probably heard of that one. And two French overseas territories: Reunion and Mayotte. First of all, all the islands have French as an official or co-official language. Later on, the British came in and made English co-official with Seychelles and Mauritius. Comoros has three official languages along with French, Comorian, and Arabic, making them the southernmost member of the Arab League. Whereas, Madagascar has their own language co-official with French, Malagasy, which is actually an Austronesian language related to languages that you can find in Indonesia and Malaysia. Let's profile each country starting with Comoros. They're made up of these three main islands and a weird rotating presidential system in which the succeeding president has to represent a different island. They have a very unique Arabic-inspired Bantu culture and they're famous for growing the very rare ylang-ylang plant, which is used in essential oils, medicines, and perfumes. Next up, Seychelles. Don't let the vast water domain fool you. This is actually the smallest country both in size and population in all of Africa. In that regard, though, they also have the highest HDI and GDP per capita in all of Africa as well. So, they're doing pretty well. The country is made up of about 115 scattered small islands in the Indian Ocean. However, the vast majority of them only live on two of them: Mahe with about 90% of the population, Praslin with 7%. Also, the world's largest nut, the coco de mer, grows on only two islands here. Next up, we got Mauritius. This is the domain of the islands that they administer. However, 98% of the population just lives on the main Mauritius island. Mauritius is unique in that it's one of the only three Hindu majority countries on Earth alongside with India and Nepal. It has a very diverse population of Europeans, Africans, Indians, Chinese, and so forth. Whereas, Mayotte is a little different. It's nickname is the Death Island because sailors that came here had a trouble getting to the island because it's surrounded completely by reefs. According to local historical accounts, this land bridge actually was traversible by foot until about 1480 when a huge storm washed away and eroded much of what was left of it. According to Hinduism, the bridge was originally built by the Hindu god Rama, employing an army of vanada, which are like humanoid monkey beings, and they built the bridge so that he could cross over to Sri Lanka to save his wife. It goes between the Radaswaram Peninsula all the way to Tail Manar, Sri Lanka. And it takes about three hours. And like here's the thing. Are you fat as hell and don't want to exercise, but you also want to lose weight? Then congratulations. There's an easy way to do that. All you have to do is go to one of Earth's gravitational anomaly zones. Yes, there are parts of this planet where depending on where you stand, the gravity is either stronger, pulling down, making you heavier, or pulling less, making you lighter. There's a number of reasons as to why this is. Usually, it's because of like the density of the material underneath the Earth's crust that goes down to the core, or because it's of the tectonic activity. Let me explain. In certain parts of the world, there are subduction zones or parts where the crust goes underneath another layer of crust. Usually, these are trenches like the Mariana Trench or the South American Peru Chile Trench. In these areas, it's dragging down. It's pulling down stronger. Therefore, you would be heavier in these areas. Otherwise, there's convergence zones where two plates are sliding against each other, creating mountain ranges. They're going upwards. They're fighting against the gravity. Therefore, you would be lighter in these areas. There's also post-glacial rebound areas where there once was a big ass piece of ice over land and then it melted and after it melts like the land goes back up. It's kind of like a sponge. When you push down on a sponge, it recoils back upwards. That's kind of like what's happening in post-glacial rebound areas. Basically, the entire Hudson Bay in Canada is a post-glacial rebound area. So, if you stand in the Hudson Bay, you would be lighter. These gravitational anomalies are measured in units called milligals, and they're very infantessimal. They only stray from the average gravitational force by like 0.01 to 0.1%. So like the most you could ever hope to gain or lose would be like onetenth of 1% of your weight. Like for every thousand kg it's like 1 kilogram. Today the strongest positive anomaly where gravitational force is the strongest would be the Mariana Trench in the Pacific. And the largest negative measurement where gravity is the weakest is in the Indian Ocean just south of Sri Lanka. Scientists say the reason is because the material underneath the Indian Ocean is probably less dense. So gravity doesn't have to work as hard. So there you go. You don't have to exercise. All you have to do is go to the Indian Ocean or like the Hudson Bay and you will completely transform yourself. For the record, I know someone in the comments is going to be like, "Well, actually, like, bro, it's a joke. Shut the up.

So, if you've ever gone to any of these four countries in Southeast Asia, you've noticed they do this very strange thing. Why do they just randomly throw these discarded floppy noodles on the walls and street signs? Those aren't noodles. Those are Southeast Asian writing systems. And today, I'm going to explain how you can tell them all apart. So, long story short, context. The Brahmic writing systems migrated out of what is now South India into what is now Southeast Asia. And from there, they evolved into all the various writing systems that you see in Southeast Asia. And today, out of all of Southeast Asia, four countries still write in their native writing systems on a national level. Yes, there are regional native writing systems, especially in like Indonesia and the Philippines, but they chose to opt for the Latin script because it wouldn't be fair to impose one ethnic group's writing system nationally over all the others. So, in order to unify them, they chose Latin. Like, not everybody in Indonesia is Javanese, so they can't make everybody write Javanese. You know what I'm saying? Anyhow, let's explain how you can tell the difference between Burmese, Lao, Thai, and Kami. That's right. It's pronounced Kamay, not Kam. The Thai alphabet looks like a bunch of snakes and each snake has like a snake head and like they twist around and kind of looks like a bunch of like U's and lowercase N's with heads. Whereas the Lao script looks like the Thai alphabet if you just like tranquilized it and it started falling asleep. Like you can compare them like a lot of the letters look the same as like Thai letters if they were just like melting in the heat and got lazier. Lao is often described as like the more simplistic minimalistic version of Thai and like with a little practice the people can read each other's scripts with context. I mean the Tao and Lie languages are already pretty mutually intelligible so they kind of just piggyback off of each other. The Kami alphabet on the other hand looks like if you took the Thai script and like smashed it down and now all the letters are like crowded, crinkled up and bent like you see a lot of these crinkled letter marks. Kami is also said to be the longest alphabet on earth. Not syllable, not abuga but alphabet. They have 74 letters and 33 consonants, 23 dependent vowels, and a bunch of extra diiocritic marks. And finally, we get to Burmese. My opinion, this is the most funl looking one because the whole writing system looks like bubbles and like alien circles. It's like it looks so cool. The reason why it's so circular is because they say long ago they would have to write on palm leaves, but the straight lines would rip the leaves. So, they had to use like circular shapes. Anyway, although the majority of people in Myanmar are Burmese, the script is also used to write the minority languages that are spoken throughout the country like Shan or Mon. And yeah, this is what it looks like. So, to summarize, snakes with heads, lazy, drunk snakes, angry, scrunched up snakes with crinkles, and alien bubbles. So, there you go. I hope you learned something. Cheers.

So if you cross this border, you would go from Afghanistan's UTC plus 430 to China's plus 8, meaning you would skip ahead 3 and 1/2 hours. Next one, the Naom corridor of Bosnian Herzgoina. So if you look at the coast of the Balkans, it looks like Croatia has the entire coast of the Adriatic Sea. But if you zoom in super close, Basin Herzgoina got a very small 11 kilometer wide coastal corridor. This small little corridor is the result of a treaty between the Ottoman Empire and the Venetian Republic and it dates back to 1699. And yeah, to this day, this is the only access they have to the coast. And finally, number one salient in my opinion, Grand Popo Benin. If you look at the very bottom of the coast between Benin and Togo, it looks like it makes sense. Yeah, it goes down. BUT THEN THEN THEY TOOK ALL THAT. I was told the reason why this exists is because there was apparently some kind of like government official that lived in Benin, but he had like a mistress in Grand Popo. So when they drew out the lines, he was like, I'm just going to take that. Honorary mention, Namibia with the Capri Strip. It's like where the Okavango Delta cuts through. But yeah, what are some other strange salients and what are your favorites? Write them in the comments.

## Story 00719
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @GeographyNow
- **Source:** yt_transcript_worldbuilding
- **Video ID:** 

my full comprehensive review of China Eastern Airlines. So, I have to start off by saying the booking process was probably the most frustrating part. Apparently, I didn't realize that they do not accept confirmation codes from thirdparty websites. So, unless you book directly from their website, you will not be able to manage your booking and add any additional requests for things like seats, meals, upgrades, or services like wheelchairs. The only other way you can contact them for these requests is by calling their service line. But unfortunately, it could take forever. I waited for over an hour and there were no answers. So, in frustration, I literally had to go to the China Eastern Airlines office in Los Angeles to see a person directly. I was lucky that my city had an office, but for other people making bookings with this airline, that may not be an option, and they'll just have to wait until they get to the airport. Upon arrival at the airport, the airplane seemed pretty clean with a complacent beige and gray interior aesthetic. It kind of reminded me of a newly bought minivan I once grew up with. I noticed there were no personal overhead air vent nozzles on this flight, only reading lights. I remember at one point it did get pretty chilly and I just had to use the blanket. The entertainment system was a bit on the older side. A touchcreen with a controller, wide selection of movies to select from. Typical Hollywood blockbusters plus a sizable emphasis on Chinese cinema because shocker, this was a Chinese airline. There was no music option though, which was a little disappointing because sometimes I like to listen to music, which helps me sleep on a flight. The flight path option was not an interactive globe, but rather an older static 2D printed airline route map, kind of like the ones I remember from the early 2000s, and it just cycles between route flight information screens. Kind of disappointing because I like playing with those map screens. I'm glad there was a game selection screen as well. I actually like to kill more time playing games on flights rather than watching movies, but these games were kind of eh classic board and card game options. Nothing super engaging. Meal time. Dinner was an option between chicken and noodles or fish and rice. I had the chicken and noodles which was served with dates and wood ear mushrooms. Overall tasted okay. Pretty much tasted like Chinese food. What can I say? Although I will say the mango mousse cake was a very delightful added touch. The bathrooms were very standard and basic. Nothing over the top or extra. The staff and crew were very nice and helpful. No complaints. They did their job. If I had to summarize, I would say the flight was incredibly okay. Honestly, if it weren't for the booking problem before all this started, I may have ranked this airline a little higher. But because of the frustration at the beginning, I'm going to have to give this airline an incredibly average 5.1 out of 10. The 0.1 because the mango mousse cake boosted them up just a little bit. Cheers.

So this right here is South America. And if we zoom in in this area just off the coast, what are these? Oh my goodness, these islands. These are the Galapagos Islands. Galapagos. You probably heard of these islands. They're famous for like biodiversity and whatever, like turtles that live to 800 billion years old. But what I find more fascinating is the civil layout of these islands. First of all, it's a volcanic archipelago of over a 100 islands, about 18 main ones, but only five are inhabited. These ones right here. The island with the largest population is this one, Santa Cruz, which has Puerto Aora, the largest city at about 18,000 people. The problem is they don't have a deep water port. So, people have to fly here. But the nearest airport is right here on Baltra Island. So, they have to go to this small island right above their main island. And they have to take a little ferry between the two islands to get there. And then they have to take this long road all the way across on the other opposite side of the island to get to Puerto Ayora. It takes like 2 hours to do the whole thing. You know, on the surface, you would probably think, okay, why does the largest town not just build an airport right next to them? Look, they have some space like right here. Why do they have to depend on that airport that's like 45 km 28 mi away all the way up in the north on a separate island? Well, there's many reasons, but for one, the Equatorian government really does not want to [Â __Â ] up the ecosystem. But 200 years ago, pirates used to use these islands and they brought over like rats and goats that ate all the plants and it messed things up already. And building a new airport would just cause a lot more ecological problems. Plus, they just don't want to induce a lot more over tourism. Plus, Balora Island already had an airport that was built by the Americans in World War II, and the area is a lot more flat and aid, so there's less species that could be disturbed by building an airport. So, they were just like, "Let's just repurpose it since it's already there." Also, despite being the largest town, Puerto Iora is not the capital. The capital is actually on this island, San Christoal to the east. Puerto Bakiro Moreno, which only has about 7,000 people. And yes, they do have their own airport right next to the town. The largest and most fascinating island, this one, Isabella, only has about 3,000 people. And the majority of them live in just one little town right here. It's called Puerto Vami, which means the city of a thousand people. This place also has some of the most fascinating landscapes like the tunnels, which are these like eroded lava field columns and arches and caves, which have their own endemic ecosystems. What are condominiums? No, I'm not talking about real estate for multiplex unit buildings. No, I'm not talking about mini condoms. Condominiums. They are places in the world that are jointly administered by two or more countries. Basically, it's the opposite of a disputed territory. It's a jointly controlled territory. Today, there are a few condominiums around the world. Some are on land, some are on water, but today we're going to talk about the ones on land. First one, let's zoom in here in West Africa between the countries of Benin and Bkina Faso. If you look right at the trioint border with Togo, there's this red triangle. That is the Koalu Triangle. Not Koala, Koalu. Basically, this area used to be a disputed territory, but then in 2008, both sides agreed to create a joint committee to administer it together. And the six or so thousand inhabitants of this area are the citizens of whatever country they had connections to prior. Next one. If you zoom in here, between France and Spain, there's a small little island between the two countries in a river called Feeasant Island. This is a funny one because it dates back to the 17th century on the Treaty of the Pyrenees. Basically, the island switches sovereignty every 6 months. So from February 1st to July 31st, it belongs to Spain. And then from August 1st to January 1st, it belongs to France. And it's just been that way for over three centuries. Going back to Africa, you see this area right in between Sudan and South Sudan. This is called Abier. Basically, after the second Sudin civil war and after South Sudan gained independence in 2011, the two sides decided to jointly administer this area in between both of them. Next one, we go to Central Asia in the Fergana Valley. We've talked about this place. This is Tor Ko between Tajjikhstan and Kyrgystan. Long story short, Tajikistan has an exclave of its own country within Kyrgystan. But the only way they can get there is by taking this roadway. So both sides, Kyrgystan and Tajikhstan jointly administer the roadway areas that connect it to its exclave just so that it makes it easier for Tajik so they don't have to like show their passport and blah blah blah. It just makes it easier. And they made it a condominium in March of 2025. And finally, the two technicalities. You could say that the entire country of Andor, this small little guy right here in the Pyrenees Mountains, that could be considered a condominium because legally according to their constitution, they are a co-principality or they have two princes and the princes are whoever is the head of state of France and whoever is the bishop of the town of Lao Duel in Spain, that guy. So, it's like two nonhereditary princes. Weird, right? And finally, you could technically say that Antarctica is a condominium because the Antarctic Treaty was signed by 29 countries agreeing that they wouldn't settle or colonize the entire continent and they would just leave it up to scientific research and so forth, even though technically today seven countries lay territorial claims to the continent, but eh, whatever. So, there you go. Condominiums, basically shared roommate situations across the

## Story 00729
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @GeographyNow
- **Source:** yt_transcript_worldbuilding
- **Video ID:** 

my full comprehensive review of Thai Airways. So, this flight was going to be a relatively shorter route from Chungdu, China to Bangkok, only about 3 hours. Upon entering, I noticed the first thing was the nice calming orchid purple interior of the upholstery. It sort of matched the maroon shirt I was wearing at the time. I don't know if I'm right or not, but it just felt a little extra spacious in the seats and plane configuration in comparison to other flights I've taken in coach. It didn't feel cramped or crowded, which I appreciated. The headrests were equipped with those adjustable neck supporters, which I really like in case I doze off. Trays were clean and orderly and accommodating. They even had those cup holders. And even though it's a little old school, I appreciate it when airlines put a little inflight magazine in the back seat pockets because I like to go to the page that shows the airlines hubs and flight path destinations. They had universal plug outlets at the back of the seats, which I really appreciated because then I could charge multiple devices instead of looking for the right cables. There were no personal air vents, only light switches, so you would have to just trust the plane's temperature system. The monitors were more updated, wide touchscreen models with a pretty nice repertoire of entertainment options. I did notice though that there was no games option. Like really, what? Seriously? For a long haul carrier, I was kind of surprised. I actually prefer games to movies on flights, so I was a little disappointed. But hey, I ended up watching the Minecraft movie. But then one thing I did notice was that there were so many announcements on the plane that interrupted my movie. And occasionally, for no reason, they would just stop all the screens and show a commercial about Thailand or asking you to join the frequent flyer club or something. It was like, "H, come on. Let me just get back to my movie." But whatever. But I will say though, the bathroom was above my expectations. In the compartment above the sink, they had everything you needed. Of little complimentary toothbrushes and paste packets. You could use them just in case and dispose them after use. I love it when airlines do this. Very considerate. But the real winner in this experience was the food. I wasn't even expecting them to feed us an entire meal since this flight was so short. I thought they would just give us like a light snack or something, but no, they went all out. Chicken prawn fried pad thai, blanched chicken with jasmine rice, and to top it off, the Thai tea mochi cake with a Thai tea creamy center as a dessert. What the? I didn't even know this existed. This was hands down one of the best economycl class meals I've ever had. Overall, after taking all the factors into consideration, the cleanliness, the entertainment, the service, the food, and everything, I'm going to give this airline an 8.2 out of 10. Definitely higher up on the airline quality indexes, if I must say. So, good job, Thai Airways.

Do these people in the hot, humid jungle have to do with these people bundled up for the freezing cold winter? Well, first off, let's explain the Southeast Asian peninsula. Now, granted, it's a very diverse place, but one of them doesn't really ethnolinguistically fit in well with the others. Like Thailand and Laos, they're basically siblings. They can understand each other. They get each other. Vietnam, Cambodia, they're always fighting each other, but technically they're long-lost cousins. But then you get to Myanmar, aka Burma, and you realize they're not even related to anybody. They're kind of like the black sheep of the Southeast Asian Peninsula. And that's because unlike all of their neighbors, they are Tibetto Burman. That's right. Burmese people are related to the Tibetans. It's speculated that about 2 to 3,000 years ago, the very first Sino Tibetan peoples migrated southwards from what is now the Tibet autonomous province of China and the Sichuan and Yunan provinces as well. And from there, a completely different northern demographic of people would have to come in and adapt to the whole new climate. Later on, this guy Anarata poured his Anaratha on the Pagan Empire, which is basically the most culturally significant empire that ever took over the Burmese areas. It was basically the beginning and predecessor to what is now the Burmese people and the Burmese culture. They brought in the stupas and Buddhism and all that stuff. Fast forward and that's kind of how Myanmar aka Burma became a thing. So the next time you see Tibetans or Burmese people, just remember they are cousins.

- **Weight:** 3

- **Video ID:** 

my full comprehensive review of Philippine Airlines. So, I recently had a layover at Manila's Nino Aino International on my way to Los Angeles, and this was my first time flying Philippine Airlines, so I was anxious to see what it was like. Side note, let me just say one thing I was a little disappointed in was the fact that the airport did not have a Jollibee in it. How the hell does the busiest airport in the Philippines not have a Jollibee? Luckily, I'm reviewing the airline, not the airport. But anyway, let me first start off by saying the best part of this flight was the staff. Filipinos are already pretty well known for their pleasant, jolly demeanor. And say what you want, the staff was a complete embodiment of that positive stereotype. Also, they are a member of the Star Alliance, which I wish I had known before my flight. Now I have to retroactively claim my flight miles. Upon entering the aircraft, I noticed the seats had a sort of muted blue color to them, which personally gave me a slight hospital room feeling. I did notice the seats had those adjustable winged headrest things. Although, I will be honest, the seats were just standard and pretty much what you would expect from a coach seat. Nothing to rave about comfort-wise. I noticed the overhead sections only had reading lights, no adjustable personal air nozzles. Everyone is given a blanket, pillow, headphones, and a small welcome pouch with a sleep mask and socks with a dental kit, which is an appreciated gesture. I noticed there were no plug outlets, though, only USBC and, oddly enough, [music] USBB outlets, which I haven't seen since like 2017, but okay. The entertainment screens were a little outdated with [music] weak monitor touchcreens that you would sometimes have to push really hard to make your selection. And if that was too much of a burden, you would have to use the controller to make your selection. The safety video was amazing, though. I loved how it incorporated various regions of the Philippines to showcase the vast and colorful regions of the country. I love it when airlines do this for their flagship airlines. It's sort of like a cool diplomatic gesture to give you [music] an insight to the nation the airline represents. Entertainment options were okay. Typical Hollywood blockbusters, plus a wide selection of mostly Asian and Filipino cinema. The flight map was not an interactive globe, but rather an older static 2D route map. Little disappointed in that because I love playing with interactive route maps. The food was actually not bad. They kept the Filipino theme. For dinner, you had a choice between beef salo and an attempted chicken picata as an alternative. I chose the beef. Thought it was decent. And for breakfast, it was a choice between chicken sig and a breakfast casserole with Hungarian sausage and egg [music] kiche, which was okay as well. Overall, the experience felt like a flight that was trying hard to appease you with the pleasant staff and service to make up for the outdated quality of the aircraft and its physical interior. For a flagship carrier, I was hoping it would have been a more praiseworthy [music] experience, but it ended up being just kind of an incredibly okay and standard flight, falling short of anything I could deem outstanding. Therefore, I'm going to have to give this airline a 5.2 out of 10.2 2 because the staff was really nice and smiley and the effort was really appreciated. But still incredibly average.

Now, some cities have wonderfully placed airports not far from their city centers. Like here's Alers and here's Alers International in Algeria. However, the airport is here. I mean, this one makes sense because, you know, it's very difficult to build airports in the incredibly narrow wedged valleys of the Himalayan mountains. You know, for centuries, Lassa had already been a developed city within the limited amount of space that they had in this area. So, if they're going to build an airport, they had to use whatever nearest flat space they could find. And apparently, this was it in the river valley. Next up, let's go to the Caribbean, the island of Dominica. So, Dominica has only one international airport, Douglas Charles, which is on the all the way furthest eastern part of the island. The problem is the main city and capital, Rozo, is on the complete opposite western side of the island. So, that means if you want to go where everything's happening, you got to drive all the way through the entire interior mountainous side of the entire island to go to Roso. Here are some places you will never see in your lifetime. First one, if we go to the Kutzk oblast in the middle of southern Siberia, you find this strange little thing in the middle of nowhere. The nearest town is like a thousand km away. The PMsky crater. Geologists are baffled as to what this discshaped geological formation even is. It's not even categorically a crater, and there's no meteorite pieces nearby. Some people say it may be geothermal activity or a natural gas explosion, but that also doesn't answer a lot of questions. The trees growing around it have irregular growth patterns. Some people say it's aliens. Who knows? Next one. If you go to the Al-Mahara Governorate of Far Eastern Yemen, you will find the well of hell or the Baroot Pit, which is this random sinkhole in the middle of the desert. This pit is in the middle of nowhere. The nearest town is about 600 miles away. It was actually known for a long time, and there are lots of legends around it, but nobody had known what was inside. It wasn't until 2021 that the Oman cave exploration team was given access to this place and were the first people ever to descend into the hole. What they discovered inside was insane. It was a completely endemic ecosystem with these translucent snakes as well as these strange things that they call cave pearls as well as the carcasses of a bunch of animals that had fallen in over the years. But yeah, the next one, Cero Elono. There's this strange sharp pyramid-shaped mountain in the middle of the Amazon rainforest of Peru. This strange pyramid-shaped mountain has only been photographed and filmed a few times from airplanes. Nobody has actually ever encountered it, let alone scaled it. It just sits alone as a huge massive megalith in the middle of the flat Amazon basin. People speculate it could possibly be an ancient pyramid that was built by the natives and just got taken over by the brush. To this day, nobody has ever actually gone up to this thing partially because it is an heavily protected area of Peru and you need permission from the local natives to get even close to this place. And finally, in the middle of the Saharan desert of southern Algeria, you find Casar Dra. this random strange weird fortress that was built in the middle of the sand. There are no direct roadways or pathways that lead up to it. It's literally just in the middle of the sand ocean. To this day, we don't know too much about this structure. It was most likely a stopover point in the Saharan trade routes. As you can see, it has double walls and a portico. At one point, the Algerians said that it was inhabited by the Jews of Timoon, but other than that, we don't really know too much about it. Was it a castle? Was it a prison? Was it a military post? Was it a trading post? We don't really know too much about it. and it just creepily sits there in the middle of nowhere. So, there you go. Those are some places you'll probably never see. If you actually do end up seeing them, then dang, you are quite the intrepid traveler. Cheers.

So, imagine a colorful ballerina, beautiful and radiant, that suddenly gets hit by a cement truck and then the truck explodes and drowns in the river. But the ballerina somehow keeps surviving and puts the tutu back on and dances. That's basically Vanuatu, a country laden with lush, vibrant landscape and nature. Yet, almost every conceivable mother nature catastrophe besides blizzards has been able to successfully strike Vanuatu. And in a weird way, that kind of plays into their culture and daily lives. First of all, separated between the Coral Sea and the Pacific Ocean, Vanuatu sits on the aptly named Vanuatu or New Hedes trench, part of the larger New Hedes plate, which is a part of the larger Pacific plate. It is a subduction zone in which the Australian plate subducts under the Pacific, making it a part of the larger ring of fire, hence making it one of the most volcanically active areas on Earth, which of course is how the islands were formed. This also means earthquakes are not uncommon. About 2,000 seismic events are reported annually, although most of them are less than five on the RTER scale. Speaking of which, there are several active above and underwater volcanoes in Vanuatu, many of which you can see clearly from satellite imagery. The largest of these is the Marum volcano on Amram Island, which has two massive calderas and a third smaller one. On top of that, Vanuatu is in the dead center of the South Pacific El Nino oscillation zone in which cyclones are likely to develop and hit any land mass in the way. So, yeah, there's that, too. But yeah, for what it's worth, to this day, Vanuatu is labeled as the most at risk nation in terms of experiencing natural disasters on Earth. As you can see, oftentimes Vanuatu can get bombarded with volcanic eruptions, which in return can be accompanied by the occasional earthquake, which in return can cause a tsunamis, which would be terrible if happening during a cyclone, which in return can cause a flooding, which in return can cause things like a mudslides. And in the rare chance they all happen at the same time, you would be witnessing a Vquig Nami floodslide clone.

my full comprehensive review of Usuzbekiststan Airways business class. So a while back Usbekistan Airways wanted to help me with my Usuzbekistan episode on my channel by sponsoring my mom and I to actually go to Usuzbekistan for the project and they hooked us up with business class. As of 2017, Usbekistan Airways has operated non-stop flights to Tash Kent from JFK airport in New York City, which is where our journey would begin. First off, upon entering the aircraft, the first thing we are greeted with were Usuzbek newspapers written in cerillic. Every guest is given a nice red pouch of toiletries and accessories like slippers and sleeping masks, even a wooden shoe horn and comb. And the toothbrush was also made of wood and I'm assuming some kind of natural hair bristles. To me, it's these subtle little details and qualities that I pay attention to and take positive note on. The seats were super comfortable and of course adjustable to extend into a bed for sleeping, which again I can't thank the airlines enough for giving my mom that experience as she needs her comfort on long haul flights. Me, I don't care. You can shove me in the underbelly with the luggage. As long as I get to my destination, I don't care. But my mom needs the comfort. Now for the food, this is where I was at a loss for words because Usuzbekistan Airways doesn't rely on the typical formulaic displays of luxury tropes like champagne and lobster. They do this very unique thing where they assert their identity, weaving their Usuzbck roots and culinary heritage into each course, and in doing so, it makes the experience way more compelling and rich. They start you off with your first appetizer courses. You can choose things like smoked fish, prawns, capers, and sour cream with traditional usuzbck meat filled samsa pastries. I freaking love those things. Otherwise, you can get a shark coochie board with various cured and smoked meats and sausages, including, yes, horse meat. They have horse meat on this flight. The unforgettable experience alone was praiseworthy enough for me. Otherwise, the main courses are succulent slabs of seasoned meat, velvety, buttery sea bass, tender roasted chicken with gravy, and of course, an endless supply of drinks and mini vodka shots. I did notice though the passengers were only confined to scheduled meal services and could not just request random orders whenever they wanted. However, to be fair, the meals were very filling and we didn't feel the need to request anything outside of the allotted meal services. As for the monitor and in-flight entertainment, this is where I will be honest and say it probably could have been better. The options and catalog were a little stale and limited. The games were very rudimentary and flat, like 2D poker. And as a former USSR nation, I guess it's not too shocking, but all of the entertainment options on this flight were either or Russian box office hits. Absolutely no western or Hollywood flicks at all. So, I decided to watch the Yuri Gagarin movie. The problem was for translation there was no subtitle option. The only thing they had was a very stoic flat monotoned dubbed voice over with one man and one woman doing all the voices for all the characters. It was like legally registered husband. I am he upset for you going into space. Foolish wife. Do not be having fearfulness. I am doing good for proletariat comrades. >> But either way in the end I have to say this was honestly one of my favorite airline experiences and I would totally go back if I could. Overall, I'm going to give Usbekistan Airways business class a 9.1 out of 10. Good job, guys.

As the name implies, it is a French overseas collectivity that is administered by France. However, they have a high degree of autonomy. They basically have self-ruule, so they can kind of manage things like their own taxation, budget, language, education, infrastructure, and labor laws. They even have their own currency. They can even sign international agreements and join IGOs as long as they notify France. Whereas France just steps in for things like defense, military, foreign policy, and justice. French Polynesia is divided into five administrative divisions with six island chains. Each cluster has their own Polynesian language. These are the island chains that have Tahiti and Bora Bora, the most famous islands in the entire collectivity. You've seen those pictures of like the lagoon with the bungalows. Yeah, that's Bora Bora. And Tahiti is the biggest of all the islands. About a third of the entire population lives in these islands. And this is like the heart and center of Tahesian/ Eastern Polynesian culture. You've probably been exposed to Tahesian culture at some point in media. This is kind of like the warrior islands. Like these people have a lot more tattoos and stone platforms and carvings. Their islands don't have a lot of reefs or lagoons, so they focus a lot more on like landbased activities and they eat a lot more pigs. In that regard, the Tuamotu peoples live mostly on flat low-lying atoles with no freshwater sources. Now the Gambier or Gambia Islands are collectively joined with Touamu as a single administrative division, but they kind of have their own distinct unique culture. Like out of all the island chains, they were the only ones that actually had like a kingdom lineagebased society ruled by a king known as an Ariki Nui. Whereas all the other island chains had a fluid chieftain system. Also, they are known for being the pearl people. They were the masters of pearl farming. And finally, the Australas. This is the coolest temperate zone of French Polynesia with seasons more similar to New Zealand. In fact, the people who are actually more culturally and linguistically related to the Mauy people of New Zealand as opposed to Tahesians. And these people were a lot more focused on land-based agriculture. So, they ate a lot more things like taro and cabbages and whatever. They ate their veggies. So, yeah. So, are we just going to completely disregard the fact that the administrative structure of the Pingualuk Republic is unlike anywhere else on Earth, yet it still kind of functions pretty well? Okay, so we all know this place is famous for its bioluminescent Namu Beat trees, as well as its Porko wrapped meat logs. But, what really sticks out and what's really unique about this place is the way how it administers [music] itself. So, first off, it's not uncommon for countries to have more than one capital. For example, here's Bolivia, they have Sucre and La Paz. I mean, South Africa has three capitals, Cape Town, Bloemfontein, and Pretoria. But, Pingualuk has five capitals with rotating executive status. Let me explain. Okay, so first off, let's zoom in here on the Tang and Restop Plateau. You'll see Pingualuk etched out here from the various rift system borders and highlands, as well as the Pekke Bay. But, in reality, the country is divided up into five autonomous zones known as Tanatiru. They all have their own capitals and parliaments, as [music] well as an Algan Atuksa, which is similar to like a royal headship, but they don't use the word kings or queens. [music] It translates to something like war destroyer hero. Anyway, each Tanatiru is in charge of a different section of legislation. [music] For example, judiciary and mining affairs, as they hold the highest reserves in the highlands, is Uatwi. Representative commons and agricultural affairs are in Intengai. Senate commons and trade, since they have the largest coastal port, are in Kenchpur. Executive and defense are in Machidwin Intidet. And all the ministries and diplomacy departments are in Uriabin, which is this little detached exclave right here in the Kaukam Biwu Highlands that were conquered by the Uriabin people in the 14th century AD. By law, the new head of state, or the Avatso Omega that represents the entire country, will come from a different Tanatiru in a rotating pattern that repeats itself every 3 years. So, that means every 15 years each Tanatiru has a term at leadership. This system generally works well for them. It has had its scandals and controversies in the past, like for example, when your Tanatiru is up for leadership and all of your candidates are terrible, then like you kind of have to deal with it. Some politicians will move to the Tanatiru next up in the rotation cycle and gain residency to sort of cheat their way into politics. But, these scandals rarely happen, and they generally tend to manage themselves pretty well. So, cheers, Pingualuk.

Oh my goodness, guys. I don't understand why you're not getting it. Okay, for the 100th time, the Samoan Fa Matai system is not that complicated. But if you need me to explain it to you one more time, fine. Technically, in Samoa, all the Samoan people are kind of like royal with potential. So everything is based off of the four paramount chieftain dynasties that created Samoa, known as the Tama Aanga. Technically, there's a fifth one for American Samoa, but that one's excluded. The head of state is the Ala Omalo, descended from these dynasties. And from the dynasties, the 16 royal families or the Angatupu were created that ruled the Itamalo or the districts of Samoa. Every town and village has a matai or chief that meets in a fono matai or chief council with other chiefs. And these chiefs come in two forms. The ali or head chief and the tulle or the orrated chief who does all the talking and announcing. Once you are a matai you are expected to hold the title until you die. Keep in mind in some cases there might be a puluu or a village custodian who helps with facilitating matai duties. From there a head chief may have a talo or daughter or female relative. She holds the important role of preparing the ava ceremony for matai events. Occasionally in rare events if a topppo is not available there may be a manaya or male version but it's almost always female. If the matai dies or somehow is not available all family members of the matai immediately become suli or candidate heirs to the matai title. From there a series of speeches and concessions begin to find out who will become the next matai. Both men and women can participate but statistically 90% of the matis have been male. From there everybody else is considered a tangatanu or tal ali or just village people that are uninterested in holding traditional titles. So basically if you want to make your way to the top in Samoa, you know, having ties to the Aangupu helps, but anything is up for grabs in a fono or matai if your falup panga is on point. And then if you have that tama title, you can secure a spot as the ala malo. Simple.

Can you actually buy a Tesla with a credit card and get paid to do it? Well, in 2018, this guy did, and he got paid about $5,000. The thing is, most credit cards earn you rewards when you use them. And this guy knew exactly how to exploit it. He was able to use the Chase, Inc. Business Preferred, which is a card that gave him three points per dollar on advertising expenses. But Tesla doesn't allow you to put the entire car on a credit card. So, he used a payment service called Plastic to charge the card and send the money to Tesla. And here's the genius part. Because the transaction went through plastic instead of Tesla, it was coded as an advertising expense, meaning he was able to get three points per dollar for the entire purchase. So even after Plastic's fee, he still earned around 180,000 points, worth up to $5,000 in free travel. But Plastic quickly caught down. They closed the loophole and unfortunately it no longer works.

## Story 01157
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @JoelBervell
- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable
- **Video ID:** 

Meet Dr. Charity Oyaji, a hematologist and researcher who is working on improving care for patients living with cickle cell disease. But on June 16th, 2025, she received a notice that her 5-year NIH grant of $750,000 was being terminated. That was despite scientific evidence and early prudent results that her work was making a huge difference for people living with cickle cell disease. The termination notice said that research with equity was harmful and had low returns on investment. Those words weren't just inaccurate, but disrespectful. Her project, Cickleofit, was the first and only exercise program designed for older patients with cickle cell disease. It was improving mobility, reducing hospital visits, and cutting health care costs. They were meant to bring to the forefront the needs of marginalized communities that have been ignored for too long. But now they're gone. And it's not just her. In March, the Trump administration began cutting NH grants dedicated towards maternal mortality, racial disparities, health equity, and more. sometimes by just scanning for words like woman, black, or race. And early career scientists, especially those from underrepresented backgrounds, are being pushed out of research altogether. In August, the Supreme Court in a 5-4 decision allowed the Trump administration to continue making these cuts to federal grants. We are watching science be silenced, not because it's ineffective, but because it dares to center marginalized voices. This isn't just a funding issue. It's a justice issue.

## Story 01158
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @JoelBervell
- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable
- **Video ID:** 

Laxmi Tapa went to the hospital in the UK with her 6-month-old son. She was concerned about blue spot markings that had become darker and some new ones that were starting to appear. So, she was referred to another hospital. But on arriving there, she was arrested by police for suspicion of bodily harm, neglect, and abuse of her baby, even though there wasn't any medical proof. I wish Latchmi's story was a one-off, but misdiagnosis of birth marks like the ones that her son had called congenital dermalanccytosis or slate gray nevi happens quite frequently. Congenital dermal melanocytosis is a type of birth mark that looks flat, gray or blue gray. They usually appear at birth or the first few weeks of life and they usually disappear as babies grow older. But on skin, especially melanated skin, they can look like bruises. And unfortunately, because in medicine, we often don't learn what skin conditions look like on darker skin tones, only on lighter skin tones, these are more likely to be misdiagnosed in people of color with darker skin tones. In Lashmy's case, she had recently moved from Nepal to the UK. And she says that doctors didn't even give her son a full exam. Instead, they immediately thought she was abusing her child, called the police, and she ended up spending 20 hours in a jail cell. It wasn't until a series of scans showed that no abuse had been happening that she was let go. But for me, all I wonder about is what biases were brought into that clinic room to make that situation happen. Moments like those make or break the trust that doctors have with their patients. The long-term effects of being locked up or reported to child services will forever impact a patient's experience with the medical
