## Story 01160
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @JoelBervell
- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable
- **Video ID:** 

A few days ago, I made this video about how race is a social construct, but a lot of people left comments like this one, and it made me realize that people really don't understand what it means to say that race is a social construct. I'm Joel, the medical myth buster, and let's break down what it actually means to say that race is a social construct. In 2003, a draft of the Human Genome Project was completed. It was a complete map of the genes that make us who we are. One of its conclusions was that a genetic marker for race didn't exist, meaning that you can't look at someone's genes and find one specific genetic marker for race. In fact, they found out there's more genetic variation within racial groups than between them. Race is a construct because there's no universal way to measure race. Sometimes we do it by skin color, sometimes we do it by hair texture. At one point in history, we divided race even within skin color, like the word mulatto, octoroon, and quadroon that was used in the 19th century. What's black in the United States is not what's black in South Africa or what's black in Brazil. And what it meant to be black in the 1940s is different than what it means to be black today. Even whiteness has evolved, too. If you were Irish or Italian arriving in the United States in the late 1800s, early 1900s, you weren't considered white. You had to prove yourself white, and that often came by proving that you weren't black. And here's something a lot of people didn't know. Up until 1999, you could only select one box in terms of your heritage on the US census. If you had a mixed racial background, you'd be forced to choose whether you were going to select your black or white. It wasn't until 2000 that you could select more than one race. But here's what's important. Although race isn't real, it doesn't mean that race doesn't have real-world effects. Race is given power through policies like redlining, actions like racism, and behaviors like implicit bias. These are physical traits to create differences between one another.

---

## Story 01161
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @JoelBervell
- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable

This doctor almost dropped out of college as an undergraduate, but instead she went on to become the first black female neurosurgeon in the United States. My name is Joel, the medical mythbuster, and welcome to episode 8 of Woke Black History. Let's talk about Dr. Alexa Kennedy. She's truly mind-boggling. Dr. Kennedy was a surgical intern at Yale New Haven Hospital in 1975, becoming the first black person and first woman to ever be enrolled in the program. But it wasn't so easy. In college, she almost dropped out as a mathematics major. She said the greatest challenge she faced as a neurosurgeon was believing that it was possible. On Dr. Kennedy's first day of residency. She was working with a patient when one of the hospital's top administrators walked by. She recalls overhearing him say, "Oh, you must be one of our hospital's new equal opportunity packages." A few years later, while working as a neurosurgeon at Children's Hospital of Philadelphia, she was rated as the top resident. Throughout her 20-year career as a pediatric neurosurgeon, Dr. Kennedy helped thousands of patients, most of them the age of 10 or younger. She also has a patent for the anti-ciphon shunt device, which takes off excess fluid from the brain. Today she continues to be an advocate for encouraging diversity in medicine. But let's hear from her ourselves. >> I think you have to not let other people define you. You have to know yourself and understand their problem versus your problem. So you can't give yourself your sense of selfworth to somebody else.

## Story 01162
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @JoelBervell
- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable
- **Video ID:** 

Imagine you're in labor in pain and contracting and then suddenly a nurse wields in a screen. On it is a Zoom meeting with a judge and lawyers. You're suddenly in court while in the hospital. That's exactly what happened to Charice Doy, a professional doula in Florida. As she was actively in labor, the hospital took her to court to try to force her to have a C-section. Here's a clip. >> It's a real judge. >> My name is Michael Khil. I'm a circuit court judge here in Jacksonville, Florida. Uh, the state of Florida has filed a petition for declaratory judgment. They're asking for an emergency C-section be granted by the court. >> I have not had a chance to be able to consult with my own legal representation and I'm trying to understand how you are taking my rights away as a patient to be able to decide my own care to give me an emergency C-section against my will. >> There's so much to talk about here. My name is Dr. Joel, the medical mythbuster, and today let's talk about what this case says about medical autonomy and reproductive justice. Charice had three prior C-section, but she wanted to try for a vaginal birth. She understood the risks and had full decision-making capacity. But the decision wasn't hers anymore because under Florida law, a court can intervene when a fetus is seen to be at risk. So, after 3 hours of testimony, the judge ruled that Doy could keep trying for vaginal birth unless there is an emergency. But in that moment, her autonomy, her right to decide what happens to her own body was overridden by the law. Overnight, doctors said that her baby's heart rate dropped for seven minutes. Doy woke up to her hospital bed being wheeled to surgery. Her baby was delivered via C-section. What's so interesting to me here in this case is the ethics because in medicine, one of the most fundamental things we're taught is autonomy. If you're an adult with decision-making capacity, you have the right to refuse treatment, surgery, blood transfusions, CPR, intubation. But pregnancy exists in a different legal and ethical space. But that doesn't happen in a vacuum. Black women in the United States are more likely to have C-sections than white women, more likely to have their pain dismissed, more likely to suffer from pregnancy related complications, more likely to die from childbirth. And up until 2021, medicine reinforced many of these biases through tools like the Vback calculator, which used race in order to predict someone's likelihood of having a successful vaginal birth after previously having a C-section. If you were black or Latina, the calculator gave you a lower chance of success and pushed providers towards offering you surgery. So, that meant black women were more likely to be told that they weren't good candidates for vaginal birth and more likely to be told to have a C-section. I look at a case like this, I have to ask, is this about safety or is this about control? Because when a court can enter a delivery room where an algorithm can decide who gets the chance at a vaginal birth, that's no longer medicine. That's institutional power. If your autonomy can be taken away at one of the most vulnerable moments of your life, then the question that I have to ask is whose body is it

## Story 01163
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @JoelBervell
- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable
- **Video ID:** 

In the 1930s, this well- re-regarded cancer researcher was accused of purposely injecting patients in Puerto Rico with cancer [music] cells. Welcome to episode four of Hidden Medical History. My name is Joel, the medical mythbuster, and let's talk about Dr. Cornelius Rhodess. Dr. Cornelius Rhodess was an oncologist who was sent by the Rockefeller Foundation to San Juan, Puerto Rico to study anemia. RHS, however, wasn't happy with where he'd been placed. One night after he left a party, he found out that his car had been vandalized. His response was to go home and write a racist letter to one of his colleagues in New York. In the letter, Roads wrote that they Puerto Ricans are beyond a doubt the dirtiest, laziest, most degenerate, and thievish race of man ever inhabiting this sphere. He then wrote, "I have done my best to further the process of extermination by killing off eight and transplanting cancer into several more." The next morning, a colleague of his found the letters and went public with them, leading to a scandal. But here's the crazy thing. Roads just left Puerto Rico to New York. [music] Roads and the American media said it was a joke, and there was an investigation that happened in Puerto Rico that found that Roads did withhold treatment for some [music] of his patients. The investigation, however, didn't find any evidence of murder. But that's not even where the story ends. After fleeing from Puerto Rico, Dr. Rhodess was inducted into the army in World War II and became a colonel. From that position, he became the chief medical officer in the chemical warfare service. In his role, he experimented with all kinds of chemical gases and other chemical weapons. Many of those experiments were on human subjects and uniformed men, 60,000 in all, and a lot of the men that were tested on were Puerto Ricans. Dr. Rhodess was the one who signed off on the ethics and medical viability of a lot of these tests. There wasn't a single test that he objected to. In one test, he asked, "Does white skin blister differently than black skin? Let's find out." Despite all of this, he still became the head of the Sloan Ketering Cancer Institute. [music] And he's been celebrated for decades as one of the forefathers of chemotherapy and celebrated as a pioneering doctor in the United States. Only recently has this hidden history been getting more attention. Follow for more.

- **Weight:** 3

Yesterday at the hospital, as I was leaving a patient's room, I got an email that I haven't been able to stop thinking about. The subject line read kidney function and the message started off with, "You don't know me, but you helped save my life." In the email, the person told me that in 2022, they found one of my videos about the GFR equation and how it was affecting black patients access to kidney transplants, all while they were just about to start diialysis. They described how they were in the hospital, barely breathing, just scrolling on their phone. At first, they were being told that they wouldn't be able to enter the transplant list for months and that they would have to lose weight. But after watching my video, they started asking questions. They advocated for themselves. They showed it to their doctors. And suddenly, things started to move. They were able to get approved for beriatric surgery and enter the transplant program that same year. And now, since November 2024, they have a kidney transplant that they got from their childhood best friend. As if that wasn't enough, yesterday one of my friends in medical school also sent me this a photo of one of my videos being used to teach a medical school class. And it just made me pause for a second and realize that on the days when this work feels heavy both on and offline, or when the system feels like it's moving too slow, or when you feel like you're not making enough of a difference, sometimes you are. It just takes time to realize

## Story 01165
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @JoelBervell
- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable
- **Video ID:** 

It's breast cancer awareness month, and here's a fact that most people don't know. Black women are 40% more likely to die from breast cancer, even though they're less likely to be diagnosed. That gap [music] isn't about biology, it's about access that's been shaped by decades of unequal investment in healthcare and exclusion from research. Every year, more than 240,000 people in the United States are diagnosed with breast cancer. One in eight women will face it in their lifetime. Screenings like mammograms don't prevent cancer, but they can catch it earlier when it's easier to treat. The problem is not everyone can get them. Across the country, there's entire counties where there's no mammography center, and many people rely on Medicaid or community clinics like Planned Parenthood in order to get screenings. But under the new federal budget, millions are expected to lose that coverage, [music] including nearly 6 million women of reproductive age who gained access to it through the ACA. [music] When screening is delayed, diagnosis comes later, and cancer at that point is usually more aggressive. For example, triple-negative breast cancer, which disproportionately impacts black women. [music] Most people should start talking to their doctor about mammograms around the age of 40, but you and your doctor should talk about what makes the most sense [music] for you. As we wear pink this month, remember that breast cancer awareness isn't just about ribbons. It's about access, [music] inclusion, and who gets the chance to thrive.

## Story 01166
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @JoelBervell
- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable
- **Video ID:** 

If you had to guess what the number one cause of disability in the United States is, what would you say? Heart disease? Cancer? It's actually arthritis. Arthritis affects nearly one in four adults. That's close to 60 million people. And this may be surprising to you, but it's not just adults. Nearly 300,000 [music] children live with what's called juvenile arthritis. Behind each of these numbers is someone struggling to work, [music] to care for their families, to go to school because of pain and stiffness. And like many other conditions, arthritis unfortunately doesn't treat everyone equally. People of color and underserved communities often experience delayed diagnoses, worse outcomes, and limited access to specialists. That means years of pain [music] before the right treatment or no treatment at all. And that's where the Arthritis Foundation comes in, funding research, fighting for better access to care, and supporting caregivers and patients across the country. So this World Arthritis Day, October 12th, I'm excited to shine a light on arthritis, the inequities still around it, and the work we still need to do together to make change. To learn more, get support, or get involved, visit arthritis.org. And let me know your own story.

Hadtie still chose to attend and made history as the first black person to win an Oscar. She paved the way for black actors and actresses who came after her in the film industry. Van taught herself to code in fortra, becoming amongst the first female coders and taught other black women how to do computer programming as well. that opened the door for generations of future programmers at NASA. ## Story 01170
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @JoelBervell
- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable
- **Video ID:** 

In 2024, a study came out showing that healthy black women were far more likely to get C-sections than white women with similar medical histories. My name is Dr. Joel, the medical mythbuster, and today let's talk about black women and C-sections. In this study, economists analyzed the medical records of more than 993,000 women who gave birth between 2008 and 2017. They found that even if a black mother and a white mother with similar medical histories saw the same doctor at the same hospital, the black mother was 20% more likely to have her baby via C-section. And while the authors of the study did offer some explanations like differences in providers, physician demand, financial incentives, and more, there's a piece that I think was missing, and that has to do with clinical algorithms like the V-back calculator and how we're taught to think about race in medicine. The Vback calculator, or vaginal birth after a C-section, is a tool used by doctors to estimate an individual's chance of successfully having a vaginal birth after previously having a C-section. The algorithm, however, had a built-in factor that automatically subtracted points if you were African-American or Hispanic. That made it less likely for these populations to be offered a vaginal birth and more likely to be offered a C-section. In some cases, selecting that you were African-American or Hispanic changed your estimate of being able to successfully have a vaginal birth by up to 20%. As you can see here, it wasn't until 20121 that race was removed as a factor from the calculator. It now uses a more objective factor, previous treatment for hypertension or high blood pressure as a metric. I think it's important to remember how in medicine we've been taught to use race-based medicine like the V-back calculator without even realizing it. We subconsciously and sometimes consciously use race as a classification for medical care. And if we're not conscious of that, it can impact the types of treatments that patients get simply because the inherent biases that exist about racial groups.

## Story 01176
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @JoelBervell
- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable
- **Video ID:** 

A lot of people have been told that extremely painful periods are normal, but they're not. And for one in 10 women, it could represent a disease, one that many patients wait up to 10 years to be diagnosed with. That condition is called endometriosis. Endometriosis happens when tissue similar to the lining of the uterus starts to grow in other organs, including the ovaries, bowels, or other places. It can cause severe pelvic pain, especially during periods, but the symptoms don't stop there. People with endometriosis often experience fatigue, bloating, nausea, painful sex, and even fertility challenges. The long history of the medical system not taking women's pain seriously, and research shows that black women are less likely to be diagnosed with endometriosis, which raises questions about how bias and access to care shapes who even gets diagnosed with this disease. The reality is this: Pain that disrupts your life is not something that should be normalized. If your periods are severely painful or something doesn't feel right, talk to a doctor, because awareness is the first step to getting people the care that they deserve.

Did you know that the first ever public ambulance system in the United States was staffed by black men? Despite its success, it wasn't expanded and its black employees were purposely pushed out. My name is Joel, the medical mythbuster, and welcome to episode 10 of Woke Black History. Let's talk about Freedom House, the first mobile emergency medical service that became a national model. In the 1960s, Pittsburgh was heavily segregated by race and ambulance systems were privatized. At that time, police used to respond to medical calls. They would pick you up, throw you in the back of the car, and rush you to the hospital. But oftent times, they were slow to respond in black communities. So, Freedom House was started in 1967, was staffed by mostly young black men from inner city Pittsburgh. The program recruited otherwise unemployable individuals and retrained them as paramedics to then serve in their communities. In the first year, they responded to more than 5,800 calls and transported more than 4,600 patients. What made the Freedom House paramedics so effective was their compassion and their strong ties to their communities, but they faced racism from hospital staff and patients in discrimination by the city government. Freedom House became a national model, but after just 8 years, it was shut down by the mayor after he instituted a city-wide ambulance system. Many of the black paramedics were purposely excluded from working in the new system. Here's John Moon, a previous Freedom House employee, discussing what happened. >> That was a systematic process designed to eliminate as many, if not all of Freedom House's employees. The goal is if you eliminate the people that made history, you essentially eliminate that history that they made. Roughly 75 to 85% of the people that came over from Freedom House was systematically eliminated.

## Story 01183
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @JoelBervell
- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable
- **Video ID:** 

black, white, Asian. Where do these racial categories even come from? Spoiler, they weren't based on biology. They were invented by doctors using science that we now know to be pseudocience. But those impacts, they're still shaping medicine today. My name is Dr. Joel, the medical mythbuster, and welcome back to episode 9 of Hidden Medical History. Today, let's talk about the scientists who defined race and how race and racism still have direct impacts on medicine today. To start, we have to go back to the 1700s and meet Swedish botnist Carlos Lanaeus. He was the same guy that we all learned classified plants and animals. But in his 10th edition of his book, System and Nature, he decided that he could also classify humans and split us into four so-called varieties. Americanis, Europius, Aphrioticus, and Aioticus. Each came with personality traits and physical descriptors, none of which were based on science. But the false stereotypes, if you pause and look at this chart, they stuck. Then came Johan Bluminbach, a German physician who in 1795 coined the term Caucasian. He believed skulls from the Caucus Mountains were the most beautiful. His five race theory laid the groundwork for ranking humanity with white Europeans conveniently on top. Other scientists came along to try and perpetuate the belief using science that white people were superior and that slavery was justified. In the 1800s, American physician Samuel Morton took it further. He collected skulls, measured their sizes, and said that larger skulls meant smarter brains and that white people had the biggest skulls. His work was embraced as proof that white people were superior, and that had deadly consequences. And despite physicians like Friedick Tedman disproving him, Morton's false conclusions spread because it supported slavery and white supremacy. [music] These myths weren't just confined to anthropology. They spread to medicine, too. And those stains are still here today. One of the clearest examples, a device called a spometer, invented in the 1840s by English surgeon John Hutchinson in order to measure lung capacity. It was originally used to assess fitness for police, military, and life insurance applicants. But when the technology spread to the Americas, doctors like Samuel Cartwright twisted it. Cartwright claimed that black people naturally had lower lung capacity and he tried to use the sperometer to prove it. was a myth that was repeated by people like President Thomas Jefferson who in notes on the state of Virginia echoed that myth and stated that working in the fields revitalized the lungs of black people. The problem is that racist assumption got baked into the device itself. Even now for decades spometry machines have had an automatic race correction that assumes that black people and Asian people have lower lung functioning. And only in 2021 was the race correction recommended to be removed. That's a direct line from 19th century pseudocience to 21st century care. And unfortunately, it's just one of many examples. The myth of racial differences wasn't accidental. It was designed to maintain inequality. Understanding that history is the first step to unlearning it today. Follow, like, and share to bust more medical myths.

---

## Story 01184
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @JoelBervell
- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable
- **Video ID:** 

Today is Junth, the day in which freedom finally reached the last group of enslaved black people in Galveston, Texas, 2 and 1/2 years after the Emancipation Proclamation. And for me, every Junth I think about delayed freedom. When the law says you're free, but the system doesn't reflect it. Because what happened in 1865 wasn't a one-time injustice. It was a blueprint. And the truth is, black communities have always had to fight for justice. And we're still confronting systems where black people are the last to know, the last to be believed, the last to benefit. Take Henry Alax, a black woman who in 1951 went to Johns Hopkins for treatment. Without her knowledge or consent, doctors took her cells, cells that were found to be immortal. And in the years after, they were taken to create vaccines for COVID research, even for cancer treatments. But her family, they didn't even find out that her cells were being used until decades later. And it wasn't until 2023 that they received any reparations. Or take the descendants of Rei Indilia, enslaved Africans photographed by Harvard scientists in the 1850s against their consent in order to substantiate racial differences. It took over 175 years for Harvard to acknowledge this injustice and to hand over these photos to the rightful place. Even in the field of medicine today, freedom is still being delayed. Whether it's the fact that black patients are still less likely to be diagnosed with dermatology conditions due to underrecognition of conditions in darker skin tones or medical algorithms that have delayed treatments like kidney transplants or lung transplants or that black pain is often undertreated or black women are still three times as likely to die during childirth. Even the small steps we've made are being rolled back right now with diversity, equity, and inclusion programs under attack or funding for NAH and HHS grants that are looking at health equity and finding solutions being axed. It's important to remember that medical freedom delayed is health denied. But Junth isn't just a day to mourn what was stolen. It's a call to reclaim what's still owed. Because the truth is, every time we force an institution to admit wrongdoing, when we demand consent, dignity, and respect, we're closing the gap between freedom and justice. And that that's worth fighting

Marian Sims have been considered the fathers of gynecology. Dr. Sims got this name for his contributions to medicine, like being the first to perform a vesco vaginal fistula surgery. He also performed one of the first gallbladder surgeries and created devices like the Sim speculum, which is still used today. Anar was only 17 when she survived 30 painful and unsuccessful surgeries. Dr. Sims held a racist notion that black people, especially black women, did not feel pain. I was tagged a lot in this video by Dr. Shannon Clark, an OBGYn, and I had to talk about it because it's the perfect example of how unethical practices can be built into medicine under the guise of training. In it, she shares a story from the '9s when she was a third-year medical student on her OB/GYN rotation. One day, she was in the operating room watching the delivery of a black woman alongside the faculty physician and a resident. After the baby was delivered, the faculty member turned to the resident and asked them if they'd ever repaired what's called a fourthderee laceration. That's the most severe type of vaginal tear that can happen during childirth. It extends through the vaginal tissue, through the paranal muscles, and into the rectum, and it requires surgical repair. While the resident said, "No, I've never repaired one before." Because this patient didn't have a fourthderee laceration, the faculty member took a pair of scissors, put one side of the blade into the vagina, the other side of the blade into the rectum, and cut a fourthderee laceration just so the resident could practice. I wish I could say I'm surprised, but the truth is that's not an isolated story. For decades, doctors have been trained to practice invasive exams like pelvic and rectal exams on unconscious patients without their knowledge or consent. A small 2003 JAMAMA study revealed that nearly 90% of medical students at one medical institution had practiced pelvic exams on women that were insticized without their explicit consent. And in 2022, a study found that nearly 67% of medical students that did an exam under anesthesia never witnessed explicit consent being taken for a medical student performing that exam. Thankfully, in 2024, guidance was sent from HHS requiring consent for exams like rectal, breast, pelvic, or prostate exams that are just for educational or training purposes. But too often in medicine, we dehumanize patients, especially black patients. Studies show that black patients are still less likely to receive the same amount of pain medication even for the same injuries as white patients. And alarmingly, some medical students still believe myths like that black people have thicker skin or that black people have less nerve endings. That's why representation in medicine matters. Why we need black and brown medical students, residents, and physicians so that we're not just in the room, but we're speaking up for our patients in these rooms. What happened in that O wasn't a teaching moment, wasn't education, it was violence. And the only way we can stop it from happening is by naming it, challenging it, and transforming the culture of medicine from the inside

## Story 01197
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @JoelBervell
- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable
- **Video ID:** 

There's a normal genetic variation in people of African and Middle Eastern descent that impacts white blood cell count and could be leading to unnecessary medical testing. My name is Joel, the Medical Myth Buster, and let's talk about the Duffy null phenotype, previously known as benign ethnic neutropenia. The Duffy null phenotype is an adaptation that helps to protect against malaria. It also causes lower neutrophil levels in people of African and Middle Eastern descent, impacting nearly 2/3 of the black population in the United States and 2/3 of people with African or Middle Eastern ancestry. Neutrophil levels are typically used to determine a person's risk of infection. Lower neutrophil levels usually means increased risk of infection or can indicate a condition like leukemia. But here's the interesting thing. Having the Duffy null phenotype means you can have a lower level of neutrophils without an increased risk of infection. Because neutrophil ranges are primarily based on data from people of European and Asian descent, what ends up happening is many Duffy null patients are referred to specialists for low neutrophil counts even though it's normal. That leads to unnecessary, expensive, painful, and invasive testing like bone marrow biopsies to make sure these people don't have blood cancers. Studies like this one from 2003 even say that this could be a reason why black patients get less chemotherapy for early stage breast cancer. Not having inclusive neutrophil counts that include the Duffy null phenotype perpetuates systemic racism. It can lead to delayed or discontinued chemotherapy medication, clinical trial exclusion, and unnecessary medical procedures. As of May 2024, the American Society of Hematology is doing research into potentially redefining the absolute neutrophil counts for pediatric and adults. The hope is that for people with this genetic profile, we can avoid any more harm.

First, in 2021, the FDA released a warning about the limitations of pulse oximters in darker skin. In 2022, an FDA panel met to review clinical data about the accuracy of pulse oximters. Studies from that year found that the overestimation of oxygen saturation led to black patients being delayed care when it came to CO 19. And in February 2024, an FDA panel met again to review the accuracy of pulse oximters. In September 2024, the FDA was supposed to release recommendations, but missed that deadline. Thankfully, [music] in January 2025, the FDA released draft guidance about how manufacturers should test and label pulse oximters. Document recommended that the devices should be tested on more people, increasing it from 10 to 150 or more, and that 25% of the participants should have darker skin tones and an increase of diversity in gender. Two, this is a guidance, and previous studies have found that other advisories about pulse oximters to manufacturers have only had a modest change in diversity. This is Minnie Lee and Mary Alice Relf. On June 13th, 1973, these two girls were sterilized without their knowledge or consent. My name's Dr. Jewel, the Medical Myth Buster, and welcome back to episode 7 of Hidden Medical History. Let's talk about Relf v. Weinberger. The Relf sisters were the youngest of six children and lived in Montgomery, Alabama. Both their parents couldn't read or write. Their family was poor and eventually moved into public housing. While living there, the family planning department began administering unapproved birth control without proper consent. On June 13th, 1973, a nurse told the Relf sisters' mother that they would need more shots. Mrs. Relf, who couldn't read or write, unknowingly signed the consent form believing that her kids were going to get more shots. The next day, their dad went to go visit his daughters in the hospital but was turned away because visiting hours were over. It wasn't until 3 days later that he saw the surgical scars on them and realized that they'd been forcibly sterilized without their consent. Thanks to a nonprofit, the Southern Poverty Law Center, the Relf sisters filed a class action lawsuit. They won and it resulted in a prohibition of the use of federal funds for involuntary sterilization. And it's thanks to that lawsuit that doctors are now required to get informed consent before sterilization procedures. Mary Alice later said, "They took something from me that I can never get back." Today, the Relf sisters are only 66 and 64.

## Story 01205
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @JoelBervell
- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable
- **Video ID:** 

Almost exactly two years ago, I was invited on the Kelly Clarkson show to talk about racism in medicine. At the time, I was still a medical student during the COVID pandemic. So, I started posting videos online about race in medicine whenever I learned something in class that didn't quite sit well with me. One of those things was an equation called the GFR equation. GFR stands for glarial ftration rate. It's essentially a function of how well your kidneys work. If your GFR is high, that means your kidneys are doing well. If your GFR is low, that means they're not doing so good. That number can often determine if you see a kidney specialist, if you get diagnosed with chronic kidney disease, and if you get a kidney transplant. But for decades, there were actually two versions of that equation. One for black patients and one for everyone else. The reasoning was that black patients were assumed to have higher muscle mass, and that would affect a lab value called creatinine. So, a race-based multiplier was added. That meant even if everything else was equal, if one patient was black and one patient was non-black, the black patient would automatically be assumed to have better kidney functioning just because of their race. And that had real consequences because it could delay care, delay referrals, and even cause patients to not get kidney transplants. So, I'm sitting there explaining this to the Kelly Clarkson, and I'm already nervous. But then something incredible happens. They brought on someone whose life had been directly impacted by a video I made talking about the GFR equation years ago. Because of a video I made, a family was able to look up the GFR equation, take it to their doctor, their doctor was able to look into it, realized it was true, and that patient got moved up 5 years on the kidney transplant list. Anise was who they brought on stage, and she was actually the sister of the person who received the kidney transplant. But the reason why she was there and not her sister was because her sister had just received a kidney. It was one of those moments that made absolutely everything feel real. And there's a letter that Anise and her sister wrote that I actually still keep with me and is on my wall. Thankfully, now the GFR equation no longer includes race as of 2021. And in 2023, the organ procurement and transportation network so that anyone that's had the racebased equation used can retrospectively have their kidney weight times adjusted. And a study that came out in March 2026 is showing that over 21,000 black Americans had their weight times adjusted because of this change. These are the types of stories that I'll always continue to share. And if this is your first time here, make sure to drop a follow, share, and remember to speak up and speak out because you never know whose life is on the other side of your

When we start to have to wonder whether care comes with consequences. So when we talk about ICE, we can't just limit conversations to borders or detention centers. ## Story 01216
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @JoelBervell
- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable
- **Video ID:** 

There's a misguided racial adjustment built into this device, a spometer, that assumes that if you're black or Asian, you automatically have lower lung capacity than any other race. My name is Dr. Joel, the medical mythbuster, and welcome back to episode two of my series reboot on racial biases in medicine. In this episode, let's talk about lung function and race-based equations. Barometry is a test that measures how much air a person can breathe out after a deep breath. It's a key tool used to diagnose conditions like asthma and COPD. It's even used to determine who qualifies for certain jobs or disability benefits. But for decades, if you were black or Asian, your test results were automatically adjusted downward. For black patients, lung values were reduced by 10 to 15%, while for Asian patients, it was reduced by between 4 to 6%. That meant what was considered abnormal for a white patient was considered normal for a black or Asian patient. That led to misdiagnosis, delayed treatments, and denied access to treatments like lung transplants. Some studies like this one even suggested that race adjustments could have exacerbated racial disparities in CO 19 recovery. And the worst part is it's built into the machine, meaning that many times doctors and technicians have no idea that their patients values are being corrected. In 2022, the Global Lung Function Initiative released raceneutral equations. [music] And in May 2023, the American Thoracic Society and European Respiratory Society issued an official statement recommending a raceneutral approach to interpreting pulmonary function tests. In 2024, a study compared data between the race neutral and race-based equations. They found that had the raceneutral equation been used earlier, more black patients would have been qualified to investigate for potential lung disease, and more would have qualified for occupations like firefighting, which require a pulmonary function test. and the annual disability payments for black veterans would have increased by more than one billion per year [music] and decreased by half a billion for white veterans. In April 2025, a new study in JAMAMA network open found that the race neutral equation improved detection of asthma in black children. It caught two to four times more asthma in black children without overdiagnosing white children. Race-based medicine assumes that biology is the cause of health disparities when in fact it's racism and structural inequities that actually do. If you learned something new, follow and share because there's a lot more myths to bust.

---

What does racial bias in medicine look like? As a first year medical student, I asked myself that question every day. And the answer I found spanned centuries. It showed up in the 1800s with gynecological experiments on enslaved black women without any anesthesia, in the 1900s with the Tuskegee syphilis study, and in 2019 when I began medical school, those biases hadn't disappeared, they'd evolved. Pulse oximters, devices we use every single day in the hospital, still do not read well on darker skin tones. Black women continue to die at triple the rate during childirth when compared to white women. And kidney algorithms like the GFR equation overestimate what a patient's kidney functioning are if you're black, leading to delays in kidney transplants. So, I picked up my phone. I started talking, not to educate, but to disrupt because silence is a pre-existing condition, too. One man reached out and told me that he challenged a pulse oximter reading, potentially saving his life. Another woman said that she saw my video and said that she should get her mold checked. It turned out to be precancerous. One other woman told me that she used my video and spoke with her doctor, was able to move up five years on the kidney transplant list. Change begins when truth is spoken aloud. And so and so today on my 30th birthday, Gemini season, but also amid DEI roll backs, cuts to NIH in HHS. This Peabody isn't just a trophy. It's a reminder that truth is the most powerful form of medicine that we have. Thank you.

This video about racial medical bias that happened to a mom and her daughter is a perfect example of how race-based medicine can lead to worse outcomes for black and latina patients. The video, a mother shares how she took her 7-year-old daughter to the pediatrician because she was noticing some symptoms that looked like early puberty. But then the doctor said if the patient had been white, they would have ordered further testing, but because the girl was black and black and Latino girls go through puberty earlier, more testing wasn't needed and they could go home. Well, understandably, mom wanted to get a second opinion from a pediatric endocrinologist. So, she asked for one. Well, a few days later, she received a call and it turns out the pediat endocrinologist had agreed with the previous doctor's opinion and said that because once again, black girls go through puberty earlier, no further testing was needed. Mom still wasn't satisfied, demanded that her daughter be seen, got an appointment, and ended up getting labs drawn. And it turned out the lab work wasn't normal. The doctor started getting an MRI, looking at the adrenal glands, and doing a whole bunch of other lab work that if mom hadn't asked for it, never would have gotten done. Medicine, we're taught race-based medicine, and it's extremely common. Even though we know race is a social construct, like I talked about in this video, there are quite literally recommendations that say black and Hispanic girls start puberty earlier and to do nothing if that happens, we never talk about all the other causes that could potentially be happening. nutrition and food insecurity, environmental racism and food deserts, endocrine disrupting hormones. Singer Childish Gambino recently announced that he had a stroke and that doctors found a hole in his heart. The wild part is the condition he most likely had is something one in four people walk around with without even knowing. My name's Dr. Jewel, the medical myth buster, and today let's talk about holes in the heart, strokes, and symptoms you should never ignore. So, what's the hidden condition that can link a heart to a stroke? It's called a PFO or a patent foramen ovale. It's a tiny flap between the chambers of your heart that's supposed to close after you're born. But for millions of people, it doesn't. Normally, your lungs act as a filter for tiny clots in your bloodstream. With a PFO, a clot can slip through that hole, go to the arterial system, and go directly to the brain. That's how what's called a paradoxical embolic stroke can happen. It's actually one of the biggest causes of stroke in younger, healthy people. People who don't smoke, who don't have high blood pressure, none of the usual risk factors. What's interesting is activities like performing, singing hard, straining, can all increase the pressure in the heart and force clots through that hole. So, it's actually not a surprise that this happened on his world tour last year. The treatment is usually a catheter procedure. Doctors go through a vein in the leg and place a small mesh device that looks like an umbrella to close the hole. No open heart surgery is required. But here's what everyone should know, the warning signs of a stroke. You can remember it the fastest with the acronym BEFAST. B balance, sudden trouble walking or dizziness. E for eyes, sudden vision loss or double vision. F for face, where one side of the face is drooping. A for arms, where you have arm or leg weakness on one side. S for slurred speech or difficulty understanding. And T, time to call 911 because minutes matter. Hopefully you learned something new because recognizing stroke symptoms can literally save a life.

We were learning about cyanosis, which is when someone doesn't have enough oxygen in their blood, and so their skin turns blue. And I remember sitting there and thinking to myself, well, my skin's not going to turn blue. What's going to happen to someone that looks like me that has darker skin? But I'm so glad I decided to raise my hand and ask the question because the professor had a great answer. He said, "You can look at your nail beds. You can look at your eyes. You can look at your mucous membranes. There's a lot of different ways to figure out if someone's cyanotic beyond skin tone." And I think true strength is really taking care of yourself and not ignoring the warning signs. >> True strength. We like that, Dr. Braval. Speaking of discussions, one of the things that I I love that you do on on Instagram, for example, is you you say the thing. You say the thing, even if it's hard to talk about. My name is Dr. Jewell, the medical myth buster, and today let's talk about black women and pain. Because as shocking as that looks, this didn't come out of nowhere. Moments like that are connected to a much longer story in American medicine. I want to start that story in the 1800s with J. Marion Sims, often called the father of modern gynecology. He conducted studies on enslaved black women without any anesthesia, justified it by saying that black women didn't feel pain in the same way as others. That lie didn't stay in the 1800s, became embedded in medical and general culture, spreading through textbooks, training, and systems. Versions of that myth still show up today. For example, a study in 2016 from the University of Virginia found that at least 50% of medical trainees believed at least one false statement about biological differences in black patients, including the idea that black people have thicker skin or feel less pain. And that's how bias works. It doesn't always show up as hatred or slurs. It shows up as delays, as disbelief, as assuming that someone is exaggerating, as telling a woman in active labor to just answer a few more questions. When she asks, "Do you treat all your patients like this or just the black ones?" she's not imagining things. She's naming a pattern that's been documented for decades. This is part of the reason why black women in the United States are three to four times more likely to die during childbirth compared to white women. Not because of biology, but because of systemic bias and delays in care. For me, this isn't about blaming one nurse in one moment. It's about understanding how the systems we built and the history that we inherited make moments like this feel normal when they're anything but. We can't fix what we refuse to see, and black women deserve a healthcare system that listens the first time.

And because there's been so many new faces here, I want to take a second to reintroduce myself and share my journey. My name is Dr. Joel Bravel. I'm an internal medicine resident doctor, but online you might know me better as a medical mythbuster. Over the past six years, I've built my platform by breaking down medical myths and exposing health inequities. My goal is to talk about the intersections of medicine, health, tech, politics, and how those intersections actually shape the care that different communities receive. But my journey here really began in medical school. One day, we were learning about a condition called cyanosis, which is when the skin turns blue. And I kept looking at my skin and saying, "My skin won't turn blue." So, I raised my hand and asked the professor, "What would cyanosis look like in someone with darker skin?" And he gave a great answer. You can look at the nail beds, you can look at the eyes, you can look at the mucous membranes. But I kept asking myself, if I hadn't asked that question, when would it have been the first time that I recognized sinosis in someone with darker skin? That moment made me realize how often we were talking about medicine without including patients that were looking like me. And it pushed me to start making 30 secondond Tik Toks about the things that I wasn't learning in medical school. Like how pulse oximters, devices that measure oxygen saturation, don't read as accurately in darker skin tones. How kidney equations and lung equations have historically diagnosed people differently based on race norming. how black women are three times as likely to die during childbirth compared to white women. Before I knew it, people were starting to let me know that my Tik Toks were being used in medical school and nursing school curriculum. In 2021, Tik Tok named me the ABS voice of change, and I started being invited to seek at places like Google, the FDA, and eventually the White House. What mattered most were the personal stories. People who said that my videos were helping them to get a cancer diagnosis, to advocate for themselves in the hospital, to even move up 5 years on the kidney transplant list. And since then, the journey has only expanded. I've had my story told in the New York Times. I've spoken on NBC and even been featured in Fox News. In 2024, I was interviewed by Kelly Clarkson on her show. And I gave an official TED talk, which you can watch online. I've spoken at the White House about health inequities. I've met with Vice President Harris about reproductive justice. And last year, I was even invited to the Nobel Peace Prize to interview the survivors of Hiroshima. And it hasn't stopped there. I host a podcast called The Dose with the Commonwealth Fund where I interview health policy experts. And I also co-created in star in an animated digital TV show for kids called The Doctor Is. Not to mention that I'm a full-time resident, meaning that a majority of my time is actually spent in the hospital taking care of patients. As you can tell, this has been such an insane journey. But the one thing I can promise is I will continue to provide evidence-based sciencebacked information, especially in an ecosystem today where it's hard to know what is truth. Every year I post a video like this from Amech, the annual medical education conference where black and brown premed and medical students gather to connect, learn, and build the future of medicine. And every year without fail, the racist comments roll in. Lower standards, drug dealers, affirmative action. Are they even qualified? Comments proving that when excellence doesn't look like what you're used to, you assume it's a mistake. So, let's talk about it. My name is Dr. Joel, the medical mythbuster, and let's talk about representation in medicine. First thing I should start with is that every doctor, no matter where they go to medical school, has to take the same step one exam, step two exam, and eventually their board exams. Quality is not lowered for any doctor. And if you made that assumption based on someone's skin color, then hint, you're racist. It's important to also understand history. In the United States, black Americans make up 13% of the population, but less than 6% of doctors. And that's intentional. It stretches back to 1910 when a report by Abraham Flexner led to the shutdown of five of the seven medical schools that trained black physicians at the time. That one report decimated the pipeline of black physicians because the number one indicator that you become a doctor is having a parent that's a doctor. It wasn't until half a century later with the Civil Rights Act of 1964 that black medical students were able to attend previously segregated medical schools. And here's the thing, representation in medicine matters beyond just face value. It's life-saving. Black patients continue to be less likely to be diagnosed accurately, less likely to be believed about their pain, more likely to die from preventable conditions. But studies like this one show that increased representation of black physicians in a county increase life expectancy in medical adherence in that county. And it doesn't stop there. We can talk about something called the GFR equation. GFR measures kidney function. Essentially, how well your kidneys work to filter out toxins. But for a long time, there's a ratio correction to the GFR equation. That multiplier assumed that all black patients had better kidney functioning than any other race. Spoiler alert, we don't. That race adjustment delayed access to care and kidney transplants for tens of thousands of black patients across the United States. It took black medical students, residents, and doctors to recognize that problem and fix it. Or we can talk about sperometry, a test that measures how much air we can breathe out in one breath. Lung function tests for a long time assumed that black and Asian patients had lower lung function than any other race. It was based on racist 19th century assumptions, not biology. And that's not medicine. That's systemic racism that's built into our tools that we use today. When it comes to clinical trials, black people, women, and other minoritized groups are still less likely to be included. That means many of the drugs that we take and the treatments that we trust are tested only on white able-bodied males. Despite there being research that shows, for example, that heart attacks present differently in women than men. That's why representation in medicine matters, why Amech matters. We're not just here to diversify medicine. We're here to decolonize it. to call out what's broken and to fix it.

They happened inside the hospital. At first, each case was treated as isolated and disconnected, but then in December 2024, three additional pre-term infants were found to have fractures that were nearly identical. At that point, the hospital paused NICU admissions and escalated the investigation. And as part of that investigation, a nurse, Aaron Stman, was placed on administrative leave. Once she was removed from patient care, the injury stopped. No new fractures, no new unexplained trauma. In January 2025, Stman was charged with felony child abuse. And by May 2025, her nursing license was suspended. But just a few days ago, on January 19th, 2026, she entered a plea deal for the injuries of the nine infants. And this is where history matters because when children show up with injuries like this and the suspected caregiver is a parent, especially a black parent, medicine moves fast. DPS is called. Medicine moves differently depending on who is suspected and whose pain is believed. This isn't speculation. When I was in medical school, I was only taught what it looks like on lighter skin tones, not on darker skin tones. See the difference? If you were to Google Lyme disease right now, almost every image you'll see is only on white skin. But what happens in real life? Studies show that underrecognition of Lyme disease causes people with darker skin to be 10% more likely to show later manifestations of the disease. When it isn't caught early, it can cause neurological problems, arthritis, even heart problems. That's why representation in medical illustrations isn't extra just diversity or being woke. It's accuracy, it's diagnosis, and sometimes it's life or death. If we can't see people in our medical system, we can't treat

In 2013, Henry Alax's family made history, reaching an agreement with the NIH that gave them more of a say in how their family's data was being used. It marked a shift in trust and transparency in science, something we're having to fight for more than ever today. For the season opener of my podcast, The Dose, I sat down with Dr. Francis Collins, former NIH director who led the human genome project to talk about that moment, politics in science, and why truth still matters. Here's a clip from the podcast where we talk about the Henrietta Lax agreement >> that ultimately got called Hila for Henrietta Alax. They're growing in my laboratory, my former laboratory right now. And that's true of many laboratories around the world. These have been the kind of cells where we've made all kinds of amazing discoveries. They've been involved in multiple Nobel Prize work because they are the way in which we really understand a lot about how a human cell does what it does. But of course [clears throat] this was done in a fashion that did not stand up to appropriate ethical norms even though at the time it was. It became clear that along the way the source of these cells was made public by an investigator at Hopkins. Um so the lax family descendants of Henrietta kids and grandkids and became identifiable. A group in Germany sequenced that genome from Hila cells and was purporting to publish a paper in it when it became clear that the family had no input to this and might potentially be at risk of having things about them revealed as blood relatives of Henrietta. So I got engaged at that point feeling like whoa wait a minute this isn't right. We did manage to slow down any public release of that information and there was another group that was also about to publish and I reached out with help from people who knew them to the Lax family and had the most amazing series of meetings in a conference room at Hopkins with multiple members of the family. It was fascinating. They were both really troubled that this had happened and that their relative Henrietta had never really been recognized for her contribution, but also that now that was known it was her that they were at risk and hadn't had the chance to have any input into this. But they wanted the work to continue. We set up a review group who would look at the applications. Two members of the Lax family were on that review group and over the course of many years, I think by now probably approved 60 or 70 applications. These two girls are perfect example of why race is a social construct. My name is Jewel, the medical myth buster, and hear me out. Lucy and Maria are sisters. In fact, they're fraternal twin sisters. But, many people would look at them and think that they're not related. In fact, they'd probably say that Lucy identifies as white and that Maria identifies as black. But, this is their mom with Lucy and Maria, who we'd probably would say is racially black. But, because these two girls could self-identify differently, they'd be treated differently by the US healthcare system. For Maria, US racial corrections would say that she should different lung, kidney, and heart functioning than her sister. That means compared to her sister, Maria's less likely to get a lung transplant, less likely to get a kidney transplant, and less likely to get a heart transplant just because of her race. The reason why this would happen is because in the United States, we use race as a biological variable, which then translates into clinical practice. Essentially, algorithms in medicine treat people differently based on their self-identified race. The problem is, even though these two sisters aren't genetically any different than any other pair of siblings, they'd be treated differently by the hospital system. And that makes no sense.

## Story 01260
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @JoelBervell
- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable
- **Video ID:** 

There's a misguided racial adjustment built into this device, a spometer, that assumes that if you're black or Asian, you automatically have lower lung capacity than any other race. My name is Dr. Joel, the medical mythbuster, and welcome back to episode two of my series reboot on racial biases in medicine. In this episode, let's talk about lung function and race-based equations. Spometry is a test that measures how much air a person can breathe out after a deep breath. It's a key tool used to diagnose conditions like asthma and COPD. It's even used to determine who qualifies for certain jobs or disability benefits. But for decades, if you were black or Asian, your test results were automatically adjusted downward. For black patients, lung values were reduced by 10 to 15%, while for Asian patients, it was reduced by between 4 to 6%. That meant what was considered abnormal for a white patient was considered normal for a black or Asian patient. That led to misdiagnosis, delayed treatments, and denied access to treatments like lung transplants. Some studies like this one even suggested that race adjustments could have exacerbated racial disparities in CO 19 recovery. And the worst part is it's built into the machine, meaning that many times doctors and technicians have no idea that their patients values are being corrected. In 2022, the Global Lung Function Initiative released raceneutral equations. And in May 2023, the American Thoracic Society and European Respiratory Society issued an official statement recommending a raceneutral approach to interpreting pulmonary function tests. In 2024, a study compared data between the race neutral and race-based equations. They found that had the raceneutral equation been used earlier, more black patients would have been qualified to investigate for potential lung disease, and more would have qualified for occupations like firefighting, which require a pulmonary function test. and the annual disability payments for black veterans would have increased by more than one billion per year and decreased by half a billion for white veterans. In April 2025, a new study in JAMAMA network open found that the race neutral equation improved detection of asthma in black children. It caught two to four times more asthma in black children without overdiagnosing white children. Race-based medicine assumes that biology is the cause of health disparities when in fact it's racism and structural inequities that actually do. If you learned something new, follow and share because there's a lot more myths to bust.

## Story 01261
- **Weight:** 3

- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable

How are some of y'all going around living life not caring what people think about you? Like truly. >> Well, in college, I learned my favorite psychology term called fundamental attribution error and it changed the way I see the world. Fundamental attribution error is a cognitive bias where we as people underemphasize the environmental or situational factors that impact another person and instead overemphasize the dispositional or personal factors. That definition can be confusing. So, here's an example. You see someone walking down the street and they trip. You can either say that person is clumsy. Or you could say, "Hey, look at that sidewalk. There's actually a bump there. That's why they tripped." I began to realize that we as humans automatically assign personality traits to another person. Someone's being rude, we automatically think they are purposely being rude to us. But what's made my life so much easier is that we all succumb to fundamental attribution error. We're not thinking about the situational events that are happening in someone else's life to explain or describe why they're acting in that specific manner. They could have recently had someone that died in their life. They could have just been having a bad day. It may have nothing to do with us as individuals. And instead of holding that against them and letting it influence my life, I try to be more thoughtful to the next person that comes along.

Many doctors still believe that black patients are less likely to feel pain based on false beliefs about skin thickness. I'm a fourth-year medical student, but online I'm better known as a medical mythbuster. For creating short-form content about racial biases in medicine and the ways that health care needs to change to be more inclusive for all populations. >> Let's talk about the racialization of x-ray radiation. Did you know that artificial intelligence can actually exacerbate health care disparities? The racial adjustment in a common prenatal screening blood test. >> I started creating content in the midst of the COVID pandemic. At the time I was thinking a lot about what it meant to be part of a field where less than 5% of physicians are black. And also what it just meant to be able to have a voice. And then I learned about pulse oximeters. This is a pulse oximeter. It measures your blood oxygen saturation level. But studies have shown that it doesn't treat all skin tones equally. A recent study found that pulse oximeter differences were associated with black, Asian, and Hispanic patients receiving less supplemental oxygen than white patients. >> so shocked that I never learned this. And so that was my first video that I posted. And within 24 hours of posting my video it got over half a million views. And there was nurses and doctors saying, "I had no idea about this disparity with pulse oximeters. Yet I use this device every single day." And on the flip side there was patients saying, "I wonder if this is why my loved one passed away." And reading those comments made me realize that we don't talk about this in medical school or anywhere else. And so for me one of the big things I'm trying to do is connect how the socio-political and economic factors of the world that impact race connect to these larger systemic issues of health. In medical school for example, I often heard that black patients are more likely to get asthma. We never talked about the socio-political reasons why. What's actually nothing to do with race, but had everything to do with the fact that redlining led to black communities to be closer to factories. And so really what I'm trying to do is highlight the research that's out there that shows that the way we're thinking about it is wrong. >> Authors estimate that without the disparity black patients receiving additional help would have increased from 17.7 to 46. >> video that I'm creating, they're all 30 to 90 seconds, but my goal is to talk as if I'm speaking to my mom, my brother, someone who I love, and I know that I would want them to have this information so that when they go to the doctor, they feel as if they have a little bit more autonomy and understanding what's happening to them.

## Story 01266
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @JoelBervell
- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable
- **Video ID:** 

For generations, doctors have been taught that the first-line treatment for all black patients that have hypertension or high blood pressure is a different medication than all other races. But as of August 2025, that's finally changed. My name is Dr. Joel, the medical mythbuster, and today, let's talk about hypertension and race-based prescribing. For the past two decades, US hypertension guidelines have told doctors like myself that black patients without other coorbidities should start with only two medication classes, calcium channel blockers or thazy diuretics instead of ACE inhibitors or ARBs, which were first line for everyone else. But in August 2025, the American College of Cardiology and American Heart Association released updated high blood pressure guidelines that no longer uses race in initial treatment decisions. Instead, they recommend choosing from any of the four firstline blood pressure medication classes that I mentioned earlier and basing that decision on a person's cardiovascular risk using something called prevent. The prevent calculator estimates a person's 10 and 30-year cardiovascular risk. That's things like heart attacks, heart failure, and stroke. And it does that by looking at your individual factors like your cholesterol, kidney functioning, smoking status, diabetes, and more. In [music] other words, treatment is based on a person's actual risk profile, not just their race. And this shift matters. Research from UCSF showed that even when doctors followed the old race-based prescribing rules, black patients still were more likely to have uncontrolled hypertension than non-black patients. That meant using race-based prescribing wasn't working. Instead, it was medication adherence, lifestyle, and diet adding a second or third drug that actually made a difference. Other studies like this one show that black people aren't a monolith and that hypertension patterns differ among people of African descent depending on geography, environment, access to care, and so much more. Race is not a proxy for biology. These new guidelines are huge because it moves us away from race as a shortcut for pharmacologic therapy. When we stop relying on race and instead understand why a disease process happens, we open the door to better, more precise care for everyone.

Cervical cancer is one of the only cancers that we can completely prevent. So why are cases going up in low-income areas? Let's talk about it. Cervical cancer is caused by HPV, the human papilloma virus. It's extremely common, but we've had a vaccine since 2006 that prevents the strains most likely to cause cancer, and it works. Countries with high HPV vaccination rates have seen servo cancer drop by as much as 85%. The CDC recommends the HPV vaccine, also known as Garcil 9, from ages 9 to 12 with catch-up vaccination up to the age of 26. Some adults up to age 45 may still benefit depending on risk. But here's where politics gets in the way. States with the lowest HPV vaccination rates now have the highest cervical cancer rates. Not because the vaccine doesn't work, but because misinformation and attacks on sex education are preventing people from getting it. And now the Trump administration is trying to defund many of the reproductive health centers that provide cervical cancer screening in the United States. That means fewer paps smears, fewer HPV tests, and fewer people protected. Cervical cancer can nearly be eradicated in our lifetime, but only if we follow the science and not the political misinformation. Vaccines save lives. Screenings save lives.

Those subsidies are set to expire at the end of 2025. And if Congress doesn't act, premiums could rise by an average of 75%. That's why record 24 million people enrolled this year, 95% of them had financial help. If subsidies disappear, millions of people will lose coverage. And that means that only the people that are sickest will be left on the plans, causing all costs to rise. But now, let's get to the myth. Undocumented immigrants are not eligible for ACA subsidies, Medicare, Medicaid, or CHIP. The only federal exception is emergency care signed into law by, get this, then President Ronald Reagan in 1986. That law is called Malaa. It says that if you're having a life-threatening condition like a stroke or a heart attack, that the hospital has to stabilize you and then bill you later. That's not free healthcare. That's the bare minimum to make sure people don't die in the emergency room. and the numbers. Less than 1% of all Medicaid spending goes towards emergency care for undocumented immigrants. That's it. A few states do use their own money to cover kids and pregnant people regardless of status. And some community clinics can provide basic care, but federally there is no free health care for undocumented people. So, what's really happening? Democrats want to extend subsidies and reverse Medicaid cuts like the new work requirements, which sounds good, but doesn't actually work. When Arkansas tried that in 2018, 18,000 people lost coverage and there was no change in the amount of people that had jobs. Republicans want to let the subsidies expire, which could leave millions of people, up to 15 million by 2027, without insurance. And they've said they don't want to negotiate until the government is back open. Here's the bigger picture. Almost 5 years ago, in December 2020, I created this viral video about a racial disparity with a device called a pulse oximeter. My name is Dr. Jewell, the Medical Myth Buster, and let's talk about racial biases with the pulse oximeter. Pulse oximeters look like this. The devices are going on your finger and they measure your blood oxygen saturation level. But studies like this one have shown that pulse oximeters are three times as likely to lead to inaccurate, overestimated oxygen saturation levels in patients with darker skin tones, meaning that patients may appear to have normal oxygen levels when it's actually dangerously low. What's crazy is for decades scientists have been publishing studies in journals like this one showing that pulse oximeters don't work well in darker skin tones. [music] In fact, this article, which says that skin pigmentation and other absorbers impact the measurement of pulse oximeters, is from 1976. Yet even now, years after I made my first video, the problem still hasn't been fixed and conversations are still ongoing. So here's some updates about what's happened up till now. First, in 2021, the FDA released a warning about the limitations of pulse oximeters in darker skin. In 2022, an FDA panel met to review clinical data about the accuracy of pulse oximeters. Studies from that year found that the overestimation of oxygen saturation led to black patients being delayed care when it came to COVID-19. And in February 2024, an FDA panel met again to review the accuracy of pulse oximeters. In September 2024, the FDA was supposed to release recommendations but missed that deadline. Thankfully, [music] in January 2025, the FDA released draft guidance about how manufacturers should test and label pulse oximeters. The document recommended that the devices should be tested on more people, increasing it from 10 to 150 or more, and that 25% of the participants should have darker skin tones, and an increase of diversity in gender. Guidance also says that manufacturers should use two different scales to measure. The first is called the Monk skin tone scale, and the second is called the individual typology angle. While this is all great news, there are some issues still. One, this doesn't apply to over-the-counter pulse oximeters, only medical-grade ones found in hospitals and clinics. Two, this is a guidance. And previous studies have found that other about pulse oximeters to manufacturers have only had a modest change in diversity. And lastly, this is still a draft, and many like myself are worried that because of the Trump administration's attacks on diversity, equity, and inclusion, that the finalized advisory won't be released. So much of medicine is built off of white, able-bodied male perspectives. It's why it's so important to understand how biases get passed down in technology, [music] passed on to patients, and then influence patient care. Be sure to follow, like, and share to stay tuned for the rest of the racial bias in medicine series. Next, I'll be doing a part two on how these blind spots got built into the pulse oximeter in the first place.

## Story 01275
- **Weight:** 3
- **Channel:** @JoelBervell
- **Source:** yt_transcript_relatable
- **Video ID:** 

In medicine, we measure everything. Blood pressure, heart rate, oxygen. When I was in medical school, I learned about something that I couldn't shake. A simple formula called the GFR equation. It was used to measure kidney function. Hidden inside it was a race adjustment. If you were black, and only if you were black, the equation increased your kidney score, assuming our bodies were different, stronger, tougher, less in need of help. That single assumption changed lives. It meant black patients were less likely to be diagnosed with chronic kidney disease, less likely to see a kidney specialist, less likely to receive a life-saving kidney transplant. I realized that racism in medicine isn't always loud. Sometimes it's written quietly into the code, the data, the design, until inequity begins to look like accuracy. When I learned this in 2019, I was a medical student. I didn't have much power, but I had a phone. So I started sharing what I was learning, posting short videos on TikTok and Instagram about how racism hides in medicine. I wasn't trying to go viral. I was trying to make people see. And somehow millions did. In 2021, a new equation was created without race. And by 2023, kidney transplant wait times for black patients were beginning to be corrected. That's why this award for conviction means so much to me. Because Muhammad Ali didn't just fight opponents, he fought systems. He refused to fight a war he knew was unjust. He battled a disease that tried to steal his voice. And through every round, in the ring, in the courts, even in his own health, he fought for the dignity of the human spirit. He used the tools of his time to confront injustice, not to run from it. And taught us all that the true measure of strength isn't how hard you hit, it's how deeply you can heal. Two years ago, a woman messaged me on Instagram. Her sister had been waiting for years for a kidney transplant. She'd seen my videos about the GFR equation and shared them with her sister, who then took them to her doctor. Her doctor looked at the videos, confirmed that they were correct, and because of that conversation, her sister was moved up five years on the kidney transplant list. That's what's possible when we choose to see fully and serve fully. When we remember that fairness isn't abstract, it's actionable. Ali once said that impossible is just a word used by those afraid to try. He proved that with his life. And tonight, in his honor, I hope to continue to prove that with mine. Thank you.
